---
title: "Glossary"
subtitle: "Plain-English definitions for every term named in this curriculum"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.preflight-check}
This glossary is the canonical, plain-English reference for every term any
lesson formally introduces in its `## Name` beat. Each entry gives:

- **Plain-English one-liner.** What the term means, in words you could say
  aloud to a friend.
- **First named in.** The lesson where this term is first defined — your
  starting point if you want the full story, the worked examples, and the
  "where this lives in the world" connections.

This page grows with the curriculum. If a term is missing from a recently
written lesson, that's a bug — please open an issue.
:::

## How to use this page

You can land on this page two ways:

1. **Navbar → Glossary.** Browse alphabetically, or use the table of contents
   on the right.
2. **Inline link from a lesson.** Many lessons cross-link to specific entries.
   Clicking returns you to the same scroll position via your browser's back
   button.

The glossary is *not* a substitute for the lessons. A definition tells you
what a thing is; a lesson teaches you why it matters. Use the **First named
in** link whenever you want the full picture.

---

## A

### Absolute extremum {#absolute-extremum}

The largest (max) or smallest (min) value of $f$ on a closed interval $[a, b]$; found by comparing $f$ at interior critical points and at the endpoints.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/06b-maxima-minima/index.qmd)

### Action value {#action-value}

$q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a]$ — expected return from taking $a$ in $s$ and following $\pi$ thereafter. Enables model-free greedy improvement (you can pick $\arg\max_a q$ without knowing the model).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 08, From State Values to Action Values](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/08-action-values/index.qmd)

### Actor-critic {#actor-critic}

A class of RL algorithms with two parameterised functions: an *actor* (policy $\pi_\theta$) and a *critic* (value $\hat v$ or $\hat q$). The critic is updated by TD; the actor is updated by policy gradient using the critic's value estimate.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 37, QAC: The Simplest Actor-Critic](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/37-actor-critic-qac/index.qmd)

### Addition {#addition}

Combining two numbers into a total — pictured on the number line as a hop to
the right by the second number's size.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Addition principle (counting) {#addition-principle-counting}

If a task can be done by *either* method A (in $m$ ways) *or* method B (in $n$ ways) and the two methods are mutually exclusive, the total number of ways is $m + n$. The "OR" companion to the multiplication principle's "AND".

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 6 — Permutations and Combinations](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/06-permutations-combinations/index.qmd)

### Addition rule (probability) {#addition-rule-probability}

$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$. The general rule for the probability of a union; the overlap subtraction vanishes when the events are mutually exclusive.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/14-probability/index.qmd)

### Adjoint of a matrix {#adjoint-matrix}

The transpose of the cofactor matrix; satisfies $A \cdot \text{adj}(A) = |A| \cdot I$ and gives the inverse via $A^{-1} = \tfrac{1}{|A|}\text{adj}(A)$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 4 — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/04b-matrix-inverse-cramers/index.qmd)

### Advantage function {#advantage-function}

$A_\pi(s, a) = q_\pi(s, a) - v_\pi(s)$ — how much better than the policy's average action $a$ is in state $s$. Used as a low-variance, unbiased substitute for $q$ in policy-gradient updates.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 38, A2C: Advantage Estimation](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/38-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### Algebraic connectivity {#algebraic-connectivity}

The second-smallest eigenvalue $\lambda_2(L)$ of the graph Laplacian — small for graphs with bottlenecks, large for robustly connected ones (Fiedler 1973).

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/14-spectral-graph-theory/index.qmd)

### Algebraic expression {#algebraic-expression}

A recipe built from numbers, variables, and operations, like $2x + 5$ or $(a + b)/2$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### Algebraic fraction {#algebraic-fraction}

An expression $P(x)/Q(x)$ where $P$ and $Q$ are polynomials and $Q$ is not the zero polynomial; the domain is all $x$ for which $Q(x) \ne 0$. Manipulated by the same rules as numerical fractions: factor and cancel, common-denominator addition, multiply across, divide by reciprocal.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Algebra Part I — Chapter 4, Algebraic fractions](lessons/tier-kiselev-1-algebra-1/04-algebraic-fractions/index.qmd)

### Analytic function {#analytic-function}

A complex function $f: D \subseteq \mathbb{C} \to \mathbb{C}$ whose derivative $f'(z)$ exists as a complex limit independent of the direction of approach.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Angle {#angle}

A measure of the turn between two lines that meet at a common point. Measured in degrees: a full turn is $360°$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Angle of depression {#angle-of-depression}

The angle between the horizontal at the observer and the line of sight downward to a lower object. Equal to the angle of elevation from the lower point (alternate-interior angles).

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/09-heights-and-distances/index.qmd)

### Angle of elevation {#angle-of-elevation}

The angle between the horizontal at the observer and the line of sight upward to a higher object.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 9 — Some Applications of Trigonometry](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/09-heights-and-distances/index.qmd)

### Antiderivative {#antiderivative}

A function whose derivative gives back the original function. If $F'(x) = f(x)$, then $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ — and so is $F + C$ for any constant.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 9 — The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/09-fundamental-theorem/index.qmd)

### Area {#area}

The size of the surface inside a flat shape, measured in square units (m², cm²).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

### Arithmetic-geometric progression {#arithmetic-geometric-progression}

A sequence whose $k$-th term is the term-by-term product of an AP and a GP: $T_k = (a + (k-1)d)\,r^{k-1}$. Summed by the "multiply by $r$ and subtract" trick, which converts it into a plain GP plus a boundary term.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 8 — Sequences and Series (8b)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/08b-series-sums/index.qmd)

### Arithmetic sequence {#arithmetic-sequence}

A sequence in which each term is the previous term plus a fixed amount, called the common difference. Example: $3, 7, 11, 15, \ldots$

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

### Asymptote {#asymptote}

A line that a curve approaches arbitrarily closely without ever touching. The hyperbola $x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1$ has asymptotes $y = \pm (b/a) x$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/10b-conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola/index.qmd)

### Axiom (common notion) {#axiom-common-notion}

A general logical principle accepted without proof, applicable beyond geometry — for example, "things equal to the same thing are equal to one another."

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid's Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/05-euclids-geometry/index.qmd)

### Axiomatic probability {#axiomatic-probability}

Probability defined by Kolmogorov's three axioms — non-negativity, $P(S) = 1$, and additivity for mutually exclusive events — rather than by counting equally-likely outcomes.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/14-probability/index.qmd)

## B

### Baird's counterexample {#bairds-counterexample}

A 7-state MDP with linear function approximation on which off-policy semi-gradient Q-learning's parameters diverge to infinity. The canonical demonstration of the deadly triad (Baird 1995).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 30, Q-Learning with FA and the Deadly Triad](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)

### Banach fixed-point theorem {#banach-fixed-point-theorem}

Every contraction on a non-empty complete metric space has a unique fixed point, reached by iteration from any starting point at geometric rate $L^n$. The workhorse for proving uniqueness of solutions to fixed-point equations across analysis, ODEs, and RL.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 11, The Banach Fixed-Point Theorem](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/11-contraction-mapping-theorem/index.qmd)

### Base {#base}

Two related meanings, both about the number that "sets the scale":

1. **Base of a place-value system.** The number on which a place-value system is built. Base 10 (everyday), base 2 (binary, used by computers), base 16 (hex, used by programmers), base 60 (Babylonian, still used for hours and minutes).

2. **Base of an exponent.** In an expression like $a^n$, the number $a$ that is being multiplied by itself.

**First named in (place-value sense):** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)
**First named in (exponent sense):** [Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)

### Baseline invariance {#baseline-invariance}

The lemma that $\mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi}[\nabla \log \pi(a \mid s) \cdot b(s)] = 0$ for any function $b(s)$ of state alone. Subtracting any state-only baseline from $q_\pi$ inside the policy gradient leaves the expected gradient unchanged but can reduce variance.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 38, A2C: Advantage Estimation](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/38-advantage-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### Basis {#basis}

A linearly independent set of vectors whose span is the whole space; equivalently, a minimal spanning set or a maximal independent set.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Bayes' theorem {#bayes-theorem}

$P(A \mid B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) / P(B)$ — the rule for flipping a conditional probability. Turns "what you can measure" into "what you actually want to know."

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)

### Bellman equation {#bellman-equation}

The recursion $V(s) = \mathbb{E}[R(s,a) + \gamma V(s')]$ defining the value of a state as immediate reward plus discounted future value; for the optimal value function the expectation is replaced by a max over actions, and the resulting operator is a $\gamma$-contraction with a unique fixed point.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 6 — Reinforcement Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)

### Bellman optimality equation {#bellman-optimality-equation}

$v^*(s) = \max_a \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a)[R(s, a, s') + \gamma v^*(s')]$. The non-linear fixed-point equation whose unique solution is the optimal state value $v^*$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 10, The Bellman Optimality Equation (BOE)](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/10-bellman-optimality-equation/index.qmd)

### Bijection {#bijection}

A function that is both one-one (injective) and onto (surjective) — a perfect pairing of inputs with outputs that has an inverse.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 1 — Relations and Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/01-relations-functions-types/index.qmd)

### Binomial distribution {#binomial-distribution}

The PMF $P(X = k) = \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ counting the number of successes in $n$ independent trials, each with probability $p$. Mean is $np$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Binomial theorem {#binomial-theorem}

The identity $(a+b)^n = \sum_{r=0}^{n} \binom{n}{r} a^{n-r} b^r$ — every term of the expansion is a binomial coefficient times a single power product.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 12 — The Binomial Theorem](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/12-binomial-theorem/index.qmd)

### Bootstrapping {#bootstrapping}

Substituting a current value-function estimate into a target instead of waiting for the realised return; the trick that turns the Bellman equation into an iterative learning rule.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 23, TD(0)](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/23-td-state-values/index.qmd)

### Borrow {#borrow}

The bookkeeping step in column-subtraction: when a column doesn't have enough to subtract from, you take ten from the column to its left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Burnside's lemma {#burnsides-lemma}

The number of orbits of a group action equals the average number of points fixed by each group element. Despite the name, due to Cauchy and Frobenius.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/03-group-actions/index.qmd)

## C

### Carry {#carry}

The bookkeeping step in column-addition: when a column's total exceeds 9, the overflow moves into the column to its left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Cauchy-Riemann equations {#cauchy-riemann-equations}

The conditions $u_x = v_y$ and $u_y = -v_x$ on the real and imaginary parts $f = u + iv$ that characterise complex differentiability.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Cauchy sequence {#cauchy-sequence}

A sequence whose terms become arbitrarily close to one another: for every $\epsilon > 0$ there exists $N$ with $|a_m - a_n| < \epsilon$ for all $m, n \ge N$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Cauchy's theorem {#cauchys-theorem}

For an analytic $f$ in a simply-connected region, $\oint_C f(z)\, dz = 0$ for every closed contour $C$. The keystone of complex analysis.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Cavalieri's principle {#cavalieris-principle}

If two solids lie between the same two parallel planes and every plane parallel to those slices both solids in regions of equal area, the two solids have equal volume. The discrete ancestor of integration by slicing.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/02-polyhedra/index.qmd)

### Central Limit Theorem {#central-limit-theorem}

For i.i.d. variables with finite variance, the standardised sample mean converges in distribution to a standard normal — regardless of the original distribution.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Chain rule {#chain-rule}

$(f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$ — derivative of a composition is "outer derivative times inner derivative."

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Chord {#chord}

A straight line segment whose two endpoints lie on a circle. The diameter is the longest chord.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd)

### Circle {#circle}

The set of points at a fixed distance (the radius) from a chosen centre.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Class number {#class-number}

The size $h_K = |\mathrm{Cl}(K)|$ of the ideal class group of a number field $K$ — equivalently, the number of equivalence classes of fractional ideals modulo principal ideals. $h_K = 1$ exactly when $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID (and a UFD); larger $h_K$ measures how badly element-level unique factorisation fails.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 12 — Algebraic Number Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/12-algebraic-number-theory/index.qmd)

### Combination {#combination}

A selection of $r$ items from $n$ where order does not matter: $\binom{n}{r} = n!/(r!(n-r)!)$. Counts the $r$-element subsets of an $n$-set.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Common difference {#common-difference}

The fixed step $d$ added to each term of an arithmetic progression to get the next term; can be positive, negative, zero, or fractional.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 5 — Arithmetic Progressions](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/05-arithmetic-progressions/index.qmd)

### Commutative {#commutative}

A property of an operation: the order of the two inputs doesn't change the answer. Addition and multiplication are commutative; subtraction and division are not.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Compactness {#compactness}

A topological tameness property: every open cover has a finite subcover. In $\mathbb{R}^n$ this is "closed and bounded" (Heine-Borel); in general it is the property that lets continuous functions attain their max and min and turns infinite arguments into finite ones.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/02-topology/index.qmd)

### Complement of an event {#complement-event}

For an event $E$ in a sample space $S$, the complement $\bar{E}$ is the event "$E$ does not happen". $P(\bar{E}) = 1 - P(E)$.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 14 — Probability](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/14-probability/index.qmd)

### Complex number {#complex-number}

A number $z = a + bi$ with $a, b \in \mathbb{R}$ and $i^2 = -1$. Lives as a point in the complex plane.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Completeness {#completeness}

A space is complete if every Cauchy sequence in it converges to a limit in the space; $\mathbb{R}$ is complete, $\mathbb{Q}$ is not.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Composite {#composite}

A whole number $\ge 2$ that is not prime — equivalently, has at least three divisors.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Compound-angle formula {#compound-angle-formula}

The master identity $\sin(A \pm B) = \sin A \cos B \pm \cos A \sin B$ (and its cosine and tangent siblings). Every double-angle, half-angle, triple-angle, sum-to-product, and product-to-sum formula descends from it.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trig Identities & Compound Angles](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/03b-trig-identities-compound-angles/index.qmd)

### Compound interest {#compound-interest}

Interest computed each period on the running total, so each period's interest itself earns interest. After $n$ years at annual rate $r$: $A = P(1 + r/100)^n$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/07-comparing-quantities/index.qmd)

### Condition number {#condition-number}

For a matrix, $\kappa(A) = \|A\| \cdot \|A^{-1}\|$. Measures how much input error a linear system $Ax = b$ amplifies in the output. Large $\kappa$ = ill-conditioned.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Conditional probability {#conditional-probability}

$P(A \mid B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$ — the probability of $A$ once you restrict the sample space to outcomes where $B$ has already happened.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 14 — Conditional Probability and Independence](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/14-conditional-probability/index.qmd)

### Cone {#cone}

A solid with a circular base and a single apex; volume $\tfrac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$, slant height $l = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2}$.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)

### Confidence interval {#confidence-interval}

A random interval whose construction catches the true parameter with a stated long-run frequency (e.g. 95%) under repeated sampling.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

### Congruence {#congruence}

Two figures are congruent if one can be moved to coincide with the other exactly — same shape *and* same size. For triangles, congruence is forced by SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or RHS.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 7 — Triangles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/07-triangles/index.qmd)

### Conjugate (surd) {#conjugate-surd}

The expression $a - \sqrt{b}$ is the conjugate of $a + \sqrt{b}$. Multiplying a binomial surd by its conjugate gives $a^2 - b$, a plain rational — the trick behind rationalising the denominator.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/01-number-systems/index.qmd)

### Continuous function {#continuous-function}

A function whose graph can be traced through a point (or interval) without lifting the pen — formally, $\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Contraction mapping {#contraction-mapping}

A map $f$ on a metric space with $d(f(x), f(y)) \le L\,d(x, y)$ for some fixed $L < 1$ — it shrinks the distance between every pair of points by at least the factor $L$. The hypothesis of the Banach fixed-point theorem.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 11, The Banach Fixed-Point Theorem](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/11-contraction-mapping-theorem/index.qmd)

### Convergence {#convergence}

A sequence $a_n$ converges to $L$ if, for every $\epsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that $n \ge N$ implies $|a_n - L| < \epsilon$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 14 — Sequences, Series, and Convergence](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/14-sequences-series-convergence/index.qmd)

### Convex function {#convex-function}

A function whose graph lies below every chord — bowl-shaped, with no internal bumps; every local minimum is automatically the global minimum.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 9 — Convex Optimisation](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/09-convex-optimization/index.qmd)

### Convolution theorem {#convolution-theorem}

Convolution in the time domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain (and vice versa) — the algorithmic miracle behind FFT-based filtering.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### Coplanar vectors {#coplanar-vectors}

Three or more vectors that lie in (or can be translated into) a single plane. Equivalently, $\vec a, \vec b, \vec c$ are coplanar iff their scalar triple product $[\vec a\ \vec b\ \vec c] = 0$ — the parallelepiped they would otherwise span has zero volume.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 10b — Dot Product, Cross Product, and Applications](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/10b-dot-cross-product-applications/index.qmd)

### Coordinate plane {#coordinate-plane}

Two number lines crossed at right angles, used to give every point in the plane a unique address $(x, y)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 4 — The Coordinate Plane](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)

### Cost price {#cost-price}

The price at which an item is bought (CP). The base for computing profit % and loss %.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/07-comparing-quantities/index.qmd)

### Cramer's rule {#cramers-rule}

For $AX = B$ with $|A| \ne 0$, each unknown is $x_i = D_i/D$, where $D_i$ replaces column $i$ of $A$ with $B$. Elegant for small systems, exponentially slow for large.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 4 — Matrix Inverse and Cramer's Rule](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/04b-matrix-inverse-cramers/index.qmd)

### Critical point {#critical-point}

A point where $f'(x) = 0$ or $f'(x)$ does not exist — the only places a smooth function can hide a local extremum.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/06-optimization/index.qmd)

### Cross entropy {#cross-entropy}

A measure $-\sum p_i \log q_i$ of how badly a predicted distribution $q$ matches a true distribution $p$; the standard classification loss in deep learning.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

### Cross product {#cross-product}

A 3D vector built from two vectors, perpendicular to both, with magnitude $|a||b|\sin\theta$ (= area of the parallelogram they span). Direction follows the right-hand rule.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)

### Cube root {#cube-root}

For a real number $a$, the unique real number $b$ with $b^3 = a$, written $\sqrt[3]{a}$. Defined for negatives too: $\sqrt[3]{-a} = -\sqrt[3]{a}$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 6 — Cubes and Cube Roots](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/06-cubes-and-cube-roots/index.qmd)

### Curl {#curl}

The local rotation of a vector field; $\nabla \times \mathbf{F}$ — a scalar in 2D, a vector along the rotation axis in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Cyclic quadrilateral {#cyclic-quadrilateral}

A quadrilateral whose four vertices all lie on a single circle. Its opposite angles are supplementary (sum to $180°$).

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd)

### Cylinder {#cylinder}

A solid swept by translating a circle of radius $r$ along a perpendicular axis of length $h$; volume $\pi r^2 h$, curved surface area $2 \pi r h$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 9 — Mensuration](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/09-mensuration/index.qmd)

## D

### DDPG (Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient) {#ddpg}

Deterministic policy gradient with a neural-network actor and critic, replay buffer, soft target updates for both actor and critic, and additive exploration noise on the behaviour policy. The continuous-control workhorse of modern deep RL (Lillicrap et al. 2015).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 40, DPG and DDPG](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/40-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### Deadly triad {#deadly-triad}

The combination of bootstrapping + off-policy sampling + function approximation. Any two are safe; all three together can cause TD-style methods to diverge, even on small finite MDPs.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 30, Q-Learning with FA and the Deadly Triad](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/30-q-learning-with-fa/index.qmd)

### Decimal {#decimal}

A number written in base-10 place value, with columns continuing to the right of a decimal point — each step right divides the value by ten.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Decimal point {#decimal-point}

The dot that marks the boundary between the ones column and the tenths column in a decimal number. It is purely a position marker, not an operator.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Deep Q-Network (DQN) {#dqn}

Q-learning with a neural-network value approximator, a replay buffer that decorrelates samples, and a target network that stabilises the bootstrap target. The 2013/2015 DeepMind algorithm that started modern deep RL.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 31, Deep Q-Network](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)

### Definite integral {#definite-integral}

The signed area between a function's graph and the x-axis over an interval $[a, b]$, written $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$. Defined as the limit of Riemann sums; by the Fundamental Theorem, it equals $F(b) - F(a)$ for any antiderivative $F$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)

### Degree of a polynomial {#degree-of-a-polynomial}

The highest power of the variable that appears in a polynomial. The degree of $3x^2 - 4x + 7$ is 2.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Derivative {#derivative}

The instantaneous rate of change of $f$ at $a$ — the limit $\lim_{h \to 0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h$, equal to the slope of the tangent line.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Determinant {#determinant}

A scalar attached to every square matrix that equals the signed $n$-volume of the parallelepiped spanned by its columns; non-zero exactly when the matrix is invertible.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 4 — Determinants](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/04a-determinants/index.qmd)

### Deterministic policy gradient {#deterministic-policy-gradient}

For a deterministic policy $\mu(s; \theta)$, $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_s[\nabla_\theta \mu(s; \theta) \nabla_a q_\mu(s, a)|_{a=\mu(s;\theta)}]$. No log-derivative trick: the chain rule differentiates straight through the action (Silver et al. 2014).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 40, DPG and DDPG](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/40-deterministic-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### Difference of squares {#difference-of-squares}

The pattern $x^2 - a^2 = (x + a)(x - a)$ — one of the most useful factoring identities in algebra.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Differentiable function {#differentiable-function}

A function for which the limit $\lim_{h \to 0} [f(a+h) - f(a)]/h$ exists at every point of its domain. Differentiable implies continuous, but not the reverse.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)

### Differential {#differential}

The infinitesimal-language symbol $df = f'(x)\,dx$ — the linear approximation written compactly.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Differential equation {#differential-equation}

An equation relating an unknown function to its own derivatives. Its solutions are *functions*, not numbers; the most famous is $y' = ky$, whose solution is exponential.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/12-differential-equations/index.qmd)

### Dihedral angle {#dihedral-angle}

The angle between two half-planes that share a common edge; measured by the planar angle of any cross-section perpendicular to the edge.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 1, Lines and planes](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/01-lines-and-planes/index.qmd)

### Dimension {#dimension}

The size (number of vectors) of any basis of a vector space; the number of independent degrees of freedom.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Direct proportion {#direct-proportion}

Two quantities $x$ and $y$ are in direct proportion if $y = kx$ for a fixed constant $k > 0$ — their ratio stays the same.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/11-direct-inverse-proportions/index.qmd)

### Direction cosines {#direction-cosines}

The cosines of the angles a line makes with the positive $x$-, $y$-, $z$-axes — written $(l, m, n) = (\cos\alpha, \cos\beta, \cos\gamma)$. They always satisfy $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$, so they encode pure direction with no magnitude.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 11a — Lines in 3D](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/11a-lines-in-3d/index.qmd)

### Direction ratios {#direction-ratios}

Any non-zero triple $(a, b, c)$ proportional to the direction cosines of a line — for instance, the components of any vector pointing along the line. Unlike direction cosines, direction ratios are not unique and do not sum-of-squares to $1$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 11a — Lines in 3D](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/11a-lines-in-3d/index.qmd)

### Directrix {#directrix}

A fixed line used in the locus definition of a parabola: every point on the parabola is equidistant from the focus and the directrix.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)

### Discount factor {#discount-factor}

The number $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ that weights future rewards in the discounted return; controls the agent's effective planning horizon $\approx 1/(1-\gamma)$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 04, Trajectories, Returns, and the Discount](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/04-trajectories-returns-episodes/index.qmd)

### Discounted return {#discounted-return}

The weighted sum of future rewards $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^\infty \gamma^k r_{t+k+1}$, with $\gamma \in [0,1)$ ensuring finiteness via the geometric series.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 04, Trajectories, Returns, and the Discount](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/04-trajectories-returns-episodes/index.qmd)

### Discriminant {#discriminant}

The expression $b^2 - 4ac$ that appears under the square root in the quadratic formula. Its sign tells you how many real roots the quadratic has.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

### Distance formula {#distance-formula}

$d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ — the Pythagorean theorem applied to the legs $|x_2 - x_1|$ and $|y_2 - y_1|$ of an axis-aligned right triangle.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)

### Distributive {#distributive}

The rule that lets you split a multiplication across an addition: $a \times (b + c) = a \times b + a \times c$. Geometrically, it's slicing a rectangle into two smaller rectangles side by side.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Divergence {#divergence}

The scalar $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{F}$ measuring how much a vector field spreads outward (positive: source) or contracts (negative: sink) at a point.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Divisibility {#divisibility}

A whole number $a$ is divisible by $b$ if $a \div b$ leaves no remainder — equivalently, if $a$ is some whole-number multiple of $b$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Division {#division}

The inverse of multiplication: given a total and one factor, find the other factor — equivalently, splitting a total into equal shares.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Domain (of a function) {#domain}

The set of permitted inputs to a function. For a real function given by a formula, the *largest* domain is the set of $x$ for which the formula is defined (no division by zero, no even root of a negative).

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/02-relations-and-functions/index.qmd)

### Dot product {#dot-product}

A scalar built from two vectors, $a \cdot b = a_1b_1 + a_2b_2 + \cdots = |a||b|\cos\theta$. Measures alignment: positive for acute angle, zero for perpendicular, negative for obtuse.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 14 — Dot and Cross Product](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/14-dot-cross-product/index.qmd)
### Dvoretzky's convergence theorem {#dvoretzky-theorem}

A stochastic-approximation theorem subsuming Robbins-Monro: the iteration $w_{k+1} = (1-\alpha_k)w_k + \beta_k T_k$ converges almost surely to $w^*$ under standard step-size, contracting-target, and bounded-variance conditions.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 21, Dvoretzky's Convergence Theorem](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/21-dvoretzky-convergence/index.qmd)


## E

### Eccentricity {#eccentricity}

A single non-negative number that classifies a conic section: $e = 0$ for a circle, $0 < e < 1$ for an ellipse, $e = 1$ for a parabola, $e > 1$ for a hyperbola. For an ellipse $e = c/a$ with $c = \sqrt{a^2 - b^2}$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Ellipse and Hyperbola)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/10b-conic-sections-ellipse-hyperbola/index.qmd)

### Eigenvalue {#eigenvalue}

A scalar $\lambda$ such that $Av = \lambda v$ has a non-zero solution; a root of $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$. The "stretch factor" the matrix applies along its preferred direction.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)

### Eigenvector {#eigenvector}

A non-zero vector that a matrix only stretches (does not rotate); $Av = \lambda v$ for the corresponding eigenvalue $\lambda$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 4 — Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)

### Elimination method {#elimination-method}

A way to solve a system of equations: scale equations so that adding or subtracting them cancels one variable, leaving a single-variable equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

### Ellipse {#ellipse}

A stretched-circle conic section — the set of points whose distances to two fixed foci sum to a constant. Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/03-conic-sections/index.qmd)

### Entropy {#entropy}

The average number of bits needed to encode one outcome of a random variable: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Maximised by uniform distributions, zero for certainties.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

### Episode {#episode}

A finite trajectory ending at a terminal state — in contrast to a continuing task that runs forever.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 04, Trajectories, Returns, and the Discount](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/04-trajectories-returns-episodes/index.qmd)

### Epsilon-delta definition {#epsilon-delta}

The rigorous definition of continuity at $a$: for every $\epsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that $|x - a| < \delta$ implies $|f(x) - f(a)| < \epsilon$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Every-visit MC {#every-visit-mc}

A Monte Carlo value-estimation rule that uses *every* occurrence of $(s, a)$ within a trajectory as a sample of $q_\pi(s, a)$. Unbiased; has lower variance per environment interaction than first-visit MC at the cost of correlated samples.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 18, MC Exploring Starts](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/18-mc-exploring-starts/index.qmd)

### Expected Sarsa {#expected-sarsa}

TD update with target $r + \gamma \sum_{a'} \pi(a' \mid s') q(s', a')$: averages $q(s', \cdot)$ under $\pi$ instead of sampling. Lower variance than Sarsa, same fixed point $q_\pi$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 27, TD Methods Unified](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/27-td-unified-view/index.qmd)

### Exploration-exploitation tradeoff {#exploration-exploitation}

The tension between gathering information about poorly-known $(s, a)$ pairs (exploration) and acting on the best current estimate (exploitation). Central problem of RL; solved by ε-greedy, softmax, UCB, Thompson sampling, intrinsic motivation, and many other mechanisms.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 19, ε-Greedy](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd)

### Exploring starts {#exploring-starts}

The assumption that each MC training episode begins from a uniformly random initial state-action pair. Guarantees every $(s, a)$ pair is visited and is sufficient for MC convergence, but is unrealistic in real systems where the agent cannot teleport.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 18, MC Exploring Starts](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/18-mc-exploring-starts/index.qmd)

### ε-greedy {#epsilon-greedy}

A behaviour policy that picks the greedy action with probability $1 - \varepsilon$ and a uniformly random action with probability $\varepsilon$. Provides exploration without exploring starts; converges to $\pi^*$ when $\varepsilon \to 0$ at a GLIE schedule.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 19, ε-Greedy](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/19-epsilon-greedy/index.qmd)

### Equation {#equation}

A statement that two expressions are equal. To solve an equation is to find the value(s) of the variable that make it true.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### Equivalence relation {#equivalence-relation}

A relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive — equivalent to a partition of the set into disjoint classes.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 1 — Relations and Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/01-relations-functions-types/index.qmd)

### Equivalent fractions {#equivalent-fractions}

Two fractions naming the same number — obtained from one another by multiplying or dividing both numerator and denominator by the same nonzero number.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Euclid's algorithm {#euclids-algorithm}

A repeated-division procedure that finds the GCD of two positive integers without ever factoring them — the oldest non-trivial algorithm still in routine use.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 1 — Real Numbers](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/01-real-numbers/index.qmd)

### Euler's identity {#eulers-identity}

The equation $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$, joining the five constants $e, i, \pi, 1, 0$ in one formula. Often called the most beautiful equation in mathematics.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Euler's polyhedron formula {#eulers-formula-polyhedra}

For every convex polyhedron with $V$ vertices, $E$ edges, and $F$ faces, $V - E + F = 2$. The first theorem of topology, proved synthetically by Cauchy via flattening one face.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 5, Regular polyhedra](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/05-regular-polyhedra/index.qmd)

### Expected value {#expected-value}

The long-run average of a random variable: $E[X] = \sum_x x \cdot P(X=x)$. Generalises the arithmetic mean to weighted outcomes.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Exponent {#exponent}

The small raised number in an expression like $a^n$ that says how many copies of the base are multiplied together.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 7 — Exponents and Powers](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/07-exponents-powers/index.qmd)

## F

### Factorial {#factorial}

The product $n! = n(n-1)(n-2) \cdots 1$, with $0! = 1$ by convention. Counts the number of orderings of $n$ distinct objects.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Factoring {#factoring}

Writing a polynomial as a product of simpler polynomials. The reverse of expanding a product. Example: $x^2 + 5x + 6 = (x + 2)(x + 3)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 12 — Factoring Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/12-factoring-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Factor theorem {#factor-theorem}

$(x - a)$ is a factor of a polynomial $p(x)$ if and only if $p(a) = 0$. The zeros of a polynomial and its linear factors are the same fact, twice.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 2 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/02-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Feasible region {#feasible-region}

The set of all points in the plane (or higher-dimensional space) that simultaneously satisfy every constraint of a system of linear inequalities. The intersection of half-planes; always convex; its corner points are where linear-programming optima are found.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 5 — Linear Inequalities](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/05-linear-inequalities/index.qmd)

### Feature vector {#feature-vector}

A $d$-dimensional encoding $\phi(s) \in \mathbb{R}^d$ of a state, used as input to a function approximator; choice of features controls the inductive bias of the learner.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 28, From Table to Function](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)

### Fermat's Last Theorem {#fermats-last-theorem}

The statement that $a^n + b^n = c^n$ has no positive integer solutions for any integer $n > 2$. Conjectured by Fermat in 1637 ("I have a truly marvellous proof which this margin is too narrow to contain") and proven by Andrew Wiles in 1995 via the Taniyama-Shimura conjecture on elliptic curves and modular forms.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 13 — Elliptic Curves](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/13-elliptic-curves/index.qmd)

### Fermat's little theorem {#fermats-little-theorem}

If $p$ is prime and $\gcd(a, p) = 1$, then $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod p$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 13 — Modular Arithmetic and the RSA Algorithm](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/13-modular-arithmetic-rsa/index.qmd)

### Field {#field}

A commutative ring with identity in which every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse — so you can *divide*. Examples: $\mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}, \mathbb{C}, \mathbb{F}_p$ for prime $p$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-rings-fields/index.qmd)

### First derivative test {#first-derivative-test}

Classify a critical point $c$ by the sign change of $f'$ across $c$: $+ \to -$ gives a local max, $- \to +$ a local min, no change neither.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/06b-maxima-minima/index.qmd)

### First principles (derivative) {#first-principles}

Computing $f'(x)$ directly from the limit definition $\lim_{h \to 0} (f(x+h) - f(x))/h$ rather than by applying differentiation rules.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 12 — Derivatives (Introduction)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/12b-derivatives-intro/index.qmd)

### First-visit MC {#first-visit-mc}

A Monte Carlo value-estimation rule that uses only the first occurrence of $(s, a)$ within each trajectory as a sample of $q_\pi(s, a)$. Unbiased and yields independent samples across trajectories, but uses fewer data points than every-visit MC.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 18, MC Exploring Starts](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/18-mc-exploring-starts/index.qmd)

### Focus (of a conic) {#focus-conic}

A fixed point used in the locus definition of a conic. A parabola has one focus; an ellipse and hyperbola have two. Distance to the focus controls the curve's defining property.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)

### Fourier series {#fourier-series}

The expression of a periodic function as an infinite sum of sines and cosines, with coefficients computed by integration against the trig basis.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 12 — Fourier Series](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/12-fourier-series/index.qmd)

### Fourier transform {#fourier-transform}

An operation $\hat f(k) = \int f(x) e^{-ikx} dx$ that re-expresses a signal as a function of frequency rather than time.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### Fraction {#fraction}

A number written $\tfrac{p}{q}$ — "p of q equal pieces of a whole". Equivalently, the answer to the division $p \div q$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Frequency {#frequency}

The number of times a value (or class of values) appears in a data set.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/04-data-handling/index.qmd)

### Frequency distribution {#frequency-distribution}

A table or chart showing how often each value (or class interval) occurs in a dataset.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 12 — Statistics](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/12-statistics/index.qmd)

### Frustum (of a cone) {#frustum}

The solid that remains when a cone is sliced parallel to its base and the top tip removed — a bucket-shape with two circular faces of different radii.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 12 — Surface Areas and Volumes](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/12-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)

### Function {#function}

A rule that assigns to each allowed input exactly one output. The same input always returns the same output.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 1 — Functions as Machines](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/01-functions-as-machines/index.qmd)

### Fundamental theorem of algebra {#fundamental-theorem-of-algebra}

Gauss (1799): every non-constant polynomial with complex coefficients has at least one complex root. Consequence: a degree-$n$ polynomial over $\mathbb{C}$ factors completely into $n$ linear factors, so it has exactly $n$ complex roots counted with multiplicity.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 2, Higher-degree equations](lessons/tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/02-higher-degree-equations/index.qmd)

### Fundamental theorem of arithmetic {#fundamental-theorem-of-arithmetic}

Every integer greater than 1 is a prime or a product of primes, and that prime factorisation is unique apart from the order of the factors.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 1 — Real Numbers](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/01-real-numbers/index.qmd)

## G

### Gaussian elimination {#gaussian-elimination}

The systematic algorithm of using three row operations to reduce a linear system's augmented matrix to triangular form, then back-substituting.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 2 — Linear Systems and Gaussian Elimination](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/02-linear-systems-gaussian/index.qmd)

### General solution (trigonometric equation) {#general-solution-trig}

The full family of solutions to a basic trig equation. For $\sin x = \sin\alpha$: $x = n\pi + (-1)^n\alpha$. For $\cos x = \cos\alpha$: $x = 2n\pi \pm \alpha$. For $\tan x = \tan\alpha$: $x = n\pi + \alpha$. ($n \in \mathbb{Z}$.)

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Equations](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/03c-trig-equations/index.qmd)

### Geometric mean {#geometric-mean}

For positive numbers $a$ and $b$, the geometric mean is $\sqrt{ab}$; for $n$ positive numbers it is the $n$-th root of their product. It is the "multiplicative average" — the constant ratio that, applied $n$ times to the first term, lands on the last in a geometric progression.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 8 — Sequences and Series (8a)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/08a-sequences-ap-gp/index.qmd)

### Geometric sequence {#geometric-sequence}

A sequence in which each term is the previous term times a fixed number, called the common ratio. Example: $2, 6, 18, 54, \ldots$

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

### Golden ratio {#golden-ratio}

The number $\varphi = (1 + \sqrt{5})/2 \approx 1.618$, equal to the limit of the ratio of successive Fibonacci numbers and the unique positive root of $x^2 = x + 1$.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 5, Sequences and Progressions](lessons/tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/05-sequences-progressions/index.qmd)

### Gradient {#gradient}

The vector of all partial derivatives of a scalar-valued function; points in the direction of steepest ascent, with magnitude equal to that maximum rate.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)

### Gradient descent {#gradient-descent}

The iterative rule $w \leftarrow w - \eta\,\nabla L(w)$: take a small step opposite the gradient to walk downhill on a loss landscape.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)

### Gradient-TD family {#gradient-td}

A class of off-policy TD algorithms (GTD, GTD2, TDC, emphatic TD) that descend [MSPBE](#mspbe) honestly by carrying a second parameter $u$ that tracks $\mathbb{E}[\phi\phi^\top]^{-1}\,\mathbb{E}[\delta\,\phi]$ on a faster timescale than the main weights $w$. Converges with linear FA off-policy where semi-gradient TD diverges; price is variance and a slower clock.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 33, Gradient-TD: GTD, GTD2, and TDC](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/33-gradient-td/index.qmd)

### Graph {#graph}

A pair $G = (V, E)$ of vertices and edges modelling pairwise relationships; edges may be directed, weighted, or neither.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Graph Laplacian {#graph-laplacian}

The matrix $L = D - A$ (degree matrix minus adjacency matrix) of a graph; symmetric, positive semi-definite, and its zero eigenvalues count connected components.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 14 — Spectral Graph Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/14-spectral-graph-theory/index.qmd)

### Greatest common divisor (GCD) {#gcd}

The largest whole number that divides both of two given whole numbers. Used for simplifying fractions and in the Euclidean algorithm.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Group {#group}

A set with a single operation satisfying four axioms — closure, associativity, identity, inverses — that abstracts every notion of symmetry, from clock arithmetic to Rubik's cube.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/01-groups/index.qmd)

### Group action {#group-action}

A way for a group $G$ to "move" the points of a set $X$, with $e \cdot x = x$ and $(gh) \cdot x = g \cdot (h \cdot x)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 3 — Group Actions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/03-group-actions/index.qmd)

## H

### Handshake lemma {#handshake-lemma}

In any graph, the sum of vertex degrees equals twice the number of edges; equivalently, the number of odd-degree vertices is even.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Heat equation {#heat-equation}

$u_t = \alpha\, u_{xx}$ (with multidimensional Laplacian in higher dimensions). Models diffusion of heat or particles. Time-irreversible — entropy increases.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Heron's formula {#herons-formula}

For a triangle with sides $a, b, c$ and semi-perimeter $s = (a+b+c)/2$, the area is $\sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$ — depending only on the three sides.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 10 — Heron's Formula](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/10-herons-formula/index.qmd)

### Hilbert space {#hilbert-space}

A complete inner-product space — the infinite-dimensional analogue of Euclidean space; the natural setting for Fourier analysis, quantum mechanics, and modern PDE theory.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### Histogram {#histogram}

A bar chart of a grouped frequency table; bars touch because the class intervals are continuous.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/04-data-handling/index.qmd)

### Hyperbola {#hyperbola}

A two-branch conic section — the set of points whose distances to two foci differ by a constant. Comet flybys trace hyperbolas.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 3 — Conic Sections](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/03-conic-sections/index.qmd)

### Hypotenuse {#hypotenuse}

The side of a right triangle opposite the right angle — always the longest side.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 10 — The Pythagorean Theorem](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)

### Hypothesis test {#hypothesis-test}

A procedure that rejects a null hypothesis $H_0$ in favour of an alternative $H_1$ when a test statistic falls in a region of low probability under $H_0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

## I

### ILATE rule {#ilate-rule}

A mnemonic for picking the $u$ in integration by parts: prefer Inverse-trig, Logarithm, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential — earlier in the list becomes $u$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 7 — Integration Techniques](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/07b-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Implicit differentiation {#implicit-differentiation}

A technique for finding $dy/dx$ from a relation $F(x, y) = 0$ by differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$ and treating $y$ as a function of $x$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)

### Importance sampling {#importance-sampling}

The identity $\mathbb{E}_{x \sim p}[f(x)] = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim q}[(p/q) f(x)]$ for densities $p, q$ with $q$ covering $p$. Reweights samples drawn under one distribution to estimate expectations under another — the bridge that makes off-policy learning work.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 39, Off-Policy Actor-Critic](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/39-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### Inclusion–exclusion {#inclusion-exclusion}

The counting rule that for two finite sets, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$, with a three-set version that adds back the triple overlap. Generalises to any finite number of sets.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 1 — Sets](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/01-sets/index.qmd)

### Increasing function {#increasing-function}

A function whose output rises whenever its input does on a given interval; equivalently $f'(x) \ge 0$ on the interval (strictly $> 0$ except at isolated points for strict increase).

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/06a-tangents-normals-rates/index.qmd)

### Indefinite integral {#indefinite-integral}

The family of all antiderivatives of $f$, written $\int f(x)\,dx = F(x) + C$, where $F' = f$ and $C$ is an arbitrary constant.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 7 — Indefinite Integrals](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/07a-indefinite-integrals/index.qmd)

### Indeterminate form {#indeterminate-form}

A symbolic expression like $0/0$ or $\infty - \infty$ that does not by itself fix the value of a limit; it signals more work is needed.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Infinite discontinuity {#infinite-discontinuity}

A point where $f$ blows up to $\pm\infty$ from at least one side; equivalent to a vertical asymptote at that point.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Continuity](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05a-continuity/index.qmd)

### Initial condition {#initial-condition}

A specified value (and possibly derivative) at a single point that pins down one solution out of the family of solutions to a differential equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 12 — Differential Equations](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/12-differential-equations/index.qmd)

### Inscribed angle theorem {#inscribed-angle-theorem}

The angle subtended by an arc of a circle at the centre is twice the angle subtended at any point on the major arc.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 9 — Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/09-circles/index.qmd)

### Integer {#integer}

A whole number — positive, negative, or zero. The set of integers is written $\mathbb{Z}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Integration by parts {#integration-by-parts}

The product rule run backwards: $\int u\,dv = uv - \int v\,du$. Useful when one factor of the integrand becomes simpler under differentiation.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/10-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Intermediate Value Theorem {#intermediate-value-theorem}

A continuous function on $[a, b]$ takes every value between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ — the basis of every bracketed root-finder.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Interval {#interval}

A contiguous chunk of the real number line between two endpoints $a < b$. A *round bracket* means the endpoint is excluded — $(a, b) = \{x : a < x < b\}$ — and a *square bracket* means it is included — $[a, b] = \{x : a \le x \le b\}$. Half-open intervals such as $[a, b)$ and $(a, b]$ include one endpoint but not the other. Infinite endpoints always take a round bracket.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 1 — Sets](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/01-sets/index.qmd)

### Inverse proportion {#inverse-proportion}

Two quantities $x$ and $y$ are in inverse proportion if $xy = k$ for a fixed constant $k > 0$ — their product stays the same.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 11 — Direct and Inverse Proportions](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/11-direct-inverse-proportions/index.qmd)

### Irrational number {#irrational-number}

A real number that cannot be written as a fraction of two integers — its decimal expansion goes on forever without ever repeating. Examples: $\sqrt{2}$, $\pi$, $e$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)

## J

### Jump discontinuity {#jump-discontinuity}

A point where the one-sided limits of $f$ both exist but disagree, so the curve "jumps" without an asymptote.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Continuity](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05a-continuity/index.qmd)

## K

*(No entries yet.)*

## L

### Lagrange's theorem {#lagranges-theorem}

In a finite group $G$, the size of every subgroup divides $|G|$ — and so does the order of every element.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 1 — Groups](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/01-groups/index.qmd)

### Laplace's equation {#laplaces-equation}

$u_{xx} + u_{yy} = 0$ (or $\nabla^2 u = 0$ in higher dimensions). Solutions are *harmonic functions*; describe steady states of heat, electrostatic potential, fluid flow potential.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Lattice {#lattice}

A discrete subgroup $L \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ of full rank — equivalently, all integer combinations $\{\sum a_i b_i : a_i \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ of a fixed basis $b_1, \ldots, b_n$. The same lattice has many bases (some "good", near-orthogonal; some "bad", skewed), and that asymmetry is the foundation of lattice-based post-quantum cryptography.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/14-lattice-crypto/index.qmd)

### Latus rectum {#latus-rectum}

The chord of a conic that passes through a focus and is perpendicular to the axis. Length is $|4p|$ for $y^2 = 4px$ and $2b^2/a$ for the standard ellipse and hyperbola.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 10 — Conic Sections (Circle and Parabola)](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/10a-conic-sections-circle-parabola/index.qmd)

### Learning rate {#learning-rate}

The step-size parameter $\eta$ in a gradient-based optimiser: too small wastes compute, too large diverges.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 10 — Gradient Methods and SGD Variants](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/10-gradient-methods/index.qmd)

### Lebesgue integral {#lebesgue-integral}

An integral built by slicing the *output* axis of a function into level sets and summing each level's measure times its value; agrees with the Riemann integral on nice functions but handles wild ones and lets you swap limits with integrals (Dominated Convergence, Monotone Convergence, Fatou's lemma).

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/01-measure-theory/index.qmd)

### Limit {#limit}

The value $f(x)$ approaches as $x$ gets arbitrarily close to $a$, regardless of whether $f(a)$ itself is defined.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Line {#line}

The path traced by moving in one fixed direction; a line segment is a finite piece of a line with two endpoints.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Linear approximation {#linear-approximation}

$f(x) \approx f(a) + f'(a)(x - a)$ — using the tangent line at $a$ as a stand-in for $f$ near $a$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Linear equation {#linear-equation}

An equation in which the unknown appears only to the first power, with no products or roots of unknowns. Its standard shape is $ax + b = c$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)

### Linear function {#linear-function}

A function of the form $y = mx + b$ whose graph is a straight line.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

### Linear function approximation {#linear-function-approximation}

A value function approximator of the form $\hat v(s; w) = \phi(s)^\top w$, linear in $w$ but possibly non-linear in $s$ via the choice of features $\phi$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 28, From Table to Function](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)

### Linear inequality {#linear-inequality}

A statement that a linear expression is less than, greater than, or equal-or- not-equal to a number. Its solution is a region of the number line, not a single value.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 3 — Linear Inequalities](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/03-linear-inequalities/index.qmd)

### Linear pair {#linear-pair}

Two adjacent angles whose non-shared sides form a straight line. A linear pair always sums to $180°$.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd)

### Log-derivative trick {#log-derivative-trick}

The identity $\nabla_\theta f(\theta) = f(\theta) \nabla_\theta \log f(\theta)$ for $f > 0$. Inside an expectation, it converts the gradient of a density into a sample-able expectation (also called the score-function estimator or REINFORCE trick).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 35, The Policy Gradient Theorem](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/35-policy-gradient-theorem/index.qmd)

### Logarithm {#logarithm}

The exponent you would have to raise a base to in order to reach a given number. So $\log_{10}(1000) = 3$ because $10^3 = 1000$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 5 — Logarithms](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/05-logarithms/index.qmd)

### Logarithmic differentiation {#logarithmic-differentiation}

Take $\log$ of both sides before differentiating; useful for $y = f(x)^{g(x)}$ and messy products and quotients.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)

## M

### Magnitude {#magnitude}

The length of a vector, computed by Pythagoras: $\|(x, y)\| = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ in 2D, $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

### Manifold hypothesis {#manifold-hypothesis}

The empirical claim that real high-dimensional data (images, text, audio) lies near a much lower-dimensional curved surface inside its ambient space; the basis for non-linear dimensionality reduction and modern generative modelling.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 5 — Manifold Methods](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/05-manifold-methods/index.qmd)

### Marked price {#marked-price}

The price displayed on an item before any discount (MP). The base for computing discount %.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/07-comparing-quantities/index.qmd)

### Markov decision process {#markov-decision-process}

The five-tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$ — states, actions, transition kernel, reward function, discount — that formalises a sequential decision-making environment with the Markov property.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 01, What is an MDP?](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/01-mdp-and-grid-world/index.qmd)

### Markov property {#markov-property}

The property that, given the present state-action pair, the future is independent of the past: $P(s_{t+1} \mid s_0, a_0, \ldots, s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t)$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 02, State Transitions and the Markov Property](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/02-state-transitions/index.qmd)

### Mathematical induction {#mathematical-induction}

A proof technique establishing $P(n)$ for all $n \ge n_0$ by proving a base case and that $P(n)$ implies $P(n+1)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Matrix {#matrix}

A rectangular array of numbers; viewed actively, an encoded linear transformation whose columns are the images of the basis vectors.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 1 — Matrices and Matrix Operations](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/01-matrices/index.qmd)

### MC Basic {#mc-basic}

The simplest model-free policy iteration: alternate (a) Monte Carlo estimation of $q_\pi(s, a)$ from $N$ trajectories starting at each $(s, a)$ pair, with (b) greedy improvement $\pi(s) \leftarrow \arg\max_a \hat q(s, a)$. Requires the exploring-starts assumption.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 17, MC Basic](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/17-mc-basic/index.qmd)

### Mean {#mean}

The "average" of a list of numbers: the sum divided by the count. The balance point of the data.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Mean deviation {#mean-deviation}

The average of the absolute deviations of a data set from a chosen centre (usually the mean or median): $\text{MD} = (1/n) \sum |x_i - \bar{x}|$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 13 — Statistics](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/13-statistics/index.qmd)

### Mean Value Theorem {#mean-value-theorem}

If $f$ is continuous on $[a,b]$ and differentiable on $(a,b)$, there exists $c \in (a,b)$ with $f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 15 — Continuity and Differentiability — Rigorous Foundation](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)

### Mean-square projected Bellman error (MSPBE) {#mspbe}

$\mathrm{MSPBE}(w) = \|\hat v_w - \Pi_\mu T_\pi \hat v_w\|_\mu^2$ — the squared $\mu$-weighted distance between the current value estimate and its [projected Bellman](#projected-bellman-operator) image. Zero exactly at the TD fixed point. The objective semi-gradient TD secretly descends and that [gradient-TD](#gradient-td) descends honestly.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 32, The Projected Bellman Operator and MSPBE](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd)

### Measure {#measure}

A rule that assigns a non-negative size to subsets of a space such that the size of a countable disjoint union equals the sum of the sizes (σ-additivity); generalises length, area, volume, and probability into one axiom.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 1 — Measure Theory and Lebesgue Integration](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/01-measure-theory/index.qmd)

### Median {#median}

The middle value of a sorted dataset (or the average of the two middle values when the count is even).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Midpoint theorem {#midpoint-theorem}

The line segment joining the midpoints of two sides of a triangle is parallel to the third side and half its length.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 8 — Quadrilaterals](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/08-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)

### Modal class {#modal-class}

For grouped data, the class interval with the highest frequency. The mode formula uses the modal class together with the frequencies of the preceding and succeeding classes.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 13 — Statistics](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/13-statistics/index.qmd)

### Modular arithmetic {#modular-arithmetic}

Arithmetic done with remainders — the system that runs clocks, days of the week, hash tables, and modern cryptography.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Mode {#mode}

The most frequently occurring value in a dataset. Useful for categorical data.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Monte Carlo estimation {#monte-carlo-estimation}

Estimating an expectation $\mathbb{E}[X]$ by averaging $n$ i.i.d. samples: $\bar X_n \to \mathbb{E}[X]$ almost surely by the law of large numbers, with standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$. The foundation of every model-free RL algorithm.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 16, Mean Estimation from Samples](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/16-mean-estimation-from-samples/index.qmd)

### Multiple integral {#multiple-integral}

A definite integral over a region in two or more dimensions, computed as iterated single integrals (Fubini's theorem) with coordinates chosen to match the region.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 6 — Multiple Integrals](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/06-multiple-integrals/index.qmd)

### Multiplication {#multiplication}

The total count of small squares in a rectangle of given width and height — equivalently, repeated addition.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 5 — Multiplication as Area](lessons/tier-1-foundations/05-multiply-as-area/index.qmd)

### Multiplication principle {#multiplication-principle}

If a process has independent steps with $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ choices each, the total number of outcomes is $n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdots n_k$ — the foundation of all counting.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Mutual information {#mutual-information}

The amount $I(X;Y)$ that knowing one random variable tells you about another; zero exactly when they are independent.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 11 — Information Theory](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/11-information-theory/index.qmd)

### Mutually exclusive events {#mutually-exclusive-events}

Two events $A$ and $B$ that cannot occur together: $A \cap B = \varnothing$. For such events $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 14 — Probability](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/14-probability/index.qmd)

## N

### n-step Sarsa {#n-step-sarsa}

TD update with target $G_t^{(n)} = r_{t+1} + \cdots + \gamma^{n-1}r_{t+n} + \gamma^n q(s_{t+n}, a_{t+n})$; the bias-variance knob between TD(0) ($n=1$) and Monte Carlo ($n=\infty$).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 25, n-Step Sarsa](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/25-n-step-sarsa/index.qmd)

### Natural exponential {#natural-exponential}

The function $e^x$, equal to its own derivative — base $e \approx 2.71828$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/05-derivatives-trig-exp-log/index.qmd)

### Natural logarithm {#natural-logarithm}

The inverse of $e^x$, written $\ln x$; its derivative is $1/x$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 5 — Derivatives of Trig, Exp, Log](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/05-derivatives-trig-exp-log/index.qmd)

### Natural number {#natural-number}

A counting number: $1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots$ — the answer to *"how many?"*. They go on without end (no largest one) and are the foundation every other kind of number is built from.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?](lessons/tier-1-foundations/01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)

### Negation {#negation}

The mirror of a number across zero. The negation of $5$ is $-5$; the negation of $-5$ is $5$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Negative number {#negative-number}

A number to the left of zero on the number line — used wherever a "reference zero" exists and you can be on either side of it.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 10 — Negative Numbers](lessons/tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)

### Newton's method {#newtons-method}

The root-finding iteration $x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)$. Converges quadratically near a simple root, given a good initial guess.
