**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Normal distribution {#normal-distribution}

The bell-curve distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with PDF $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)}$; the universal limiting distribution of sums of many independent random variables.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)

### Normal line {#normal-line}

The line through a point on a curve that is perpendicular to the tangent line there; slope $= -1/f'(a)$ when $f'(a) \ne 0$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Tangents, Normals, Rates)](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/06a-tangents-normals-rates/index.qmd)

### Normal vector {#normal-vector}

A vector perpendicular to a plane (or to a curve/surface at a point). A plane is fully described by any point on it together with its normal.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 11 — Planes in 3D](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/11b-planes-in-3d/index.qmd)

### Number {#number}

A quantity — the answer to *"how many?"* or *"how much?"*. The shapes we write on paper (`3`, `12`, `1000`, `III`, `三`) are *names* for numbers, not the numbers themselves; the same number can have many names across cultures and notations.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 1 — What is a Number?](lessons/tier-1-foundations/01-what-is-a-number/index.qmd)

### Number line {#number-line}

A straight line on which every number has a single home — zero in the middle, larger numbers to the right, smaller (and negative) numbers to the left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line](lessons/tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)

## O

### Octant {#octant}

One of the eight regions into which the three coordinate planes divide 3D space, named by a sign-pattern $(\pm, \pm, \pm)$ on the coordinates.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 11 — Introduction to Three-Dimensional Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/11-three-d-geometry-intro/index.qmd)

### Off-policy {#off-policy}

A learning algorithm whose target depends on a policy different from the one generating data. Only coverage of all $(s, a)$ pairs is required for convergence.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 26, Q-Learning](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/26-q-learning/index.qmd)

### Off-policy actor-critic {#off-policy-actor-critic}

Actor-critic where data is generated by a behaviour policy $\beta$ different from the target policy $\pi_\theta$. The actor's gradient is reweighted by importance ratios $\pi_\theta(a \mid s) / \beta(a \mid s)$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 39, Off-Policy Actor-Critic](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/39-off-policy-actor-critic/index.qmd)

### On-policy {#on-policy}

A learning algorithm whose target uses actions sampled from the same policy that generated the data; learns $q_\pi$ for that policy.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 24, Sarsa](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/24-sarsa/index.qmd)

### One-sided limit {#one-sided-limit}

The value $f(x)$ approaches as $x$ tends to $a$ from one side only — written $\lim_{x \to a^+}$ (right) or $\lim_{x \to a^-}$ (left).

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 1 — Limits](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/01-limits/index.qmd)

### Optimal policy {#optimal-policy}

A policy $\pi^*$ achieving $v_{\pi^*}(s) = v^*(s)$ at every state. Exists (Bellman's theorem) and can be chosen deterministic stationary, but is not unique when greedy ties occur.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 09, Optimal Policies and Optimal Values](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/09-optimal-policies/index.qmd)

### Optimal state value {#optimal-state-value}

$v^*(s) = \max_\pi v_\pi(s)$ — the largest expected return achievable from $s$ over all policies. Unique on a finite MDP with $\gamma < 1$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 09, Optimal Policies and Optimal Values](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/09-optimal-policies/index.qmd)

### Optimisation {#optimisation}

The process of finding the input that maximises or minimises an objective function, typically by setting the derivative to zero and classifying the critical points.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 6 — Application of Derivatives (Maxima and Minima)](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/06b-maxima-minima/index.qmd)

### Order of a numerical method {#order-of-a-numerical-method}

The exponent $p$ such that the per-step error scales as $O(h^p)$. Halving $h$ multiplies accuracy by $2^p$. Euler $p=1$, trapezoidal $p=2$, RK4 / Simpson $p=4$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 8 — Numerical Methods](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/08-numerical-methods/index.qmd)

### Order of magnitude {#order-of-magnitude}

The exponent of 10 when a number is in scientific notation — how many tens it would take to multiply up to that size.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/08-scientific-notation/index.qmd)

### Ordered pair {#ordered-pair}

A pair $(x, y)$ in which the order of the entries matters: $(2, 3)$ and $(3, 2)$ are different ordered pairs and name different points on the plane.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)

### Origin {#origin}

The point on the number line (or the coordinate plane) we choose to call zero — the starting place from which every other number is measured.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 2 — The Number Line](lessons/tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd)

## P

### PAC learning {#pac-learning}

"Probably Approximately Correct": a class is PAC learnable if some algorithm, given polynomially many i.i.d. samples in $1/\epsilon$ and $1/\delta$, returns a hypothesis whose error is at most $\epsilon$ with confidence at least $1 - \delta$.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/04-statistical-learning-theory/index.qmd)

### Parabola {#parabola}

The conic section formed by slicing a cone parallel to one of its sides; the U-shaped graph of any quadratic. Equidistant from a single focus and a directrix line.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 2 — Parabolas — Graphing Quadratics](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/02-parabola-graphing/index.qmd)

### Parallel postulate {#parallel-postulate}

Euclid's fifth postulate. Playfair's form: through a point not on a given line, exactly one line parallel to it exists. Replacing it produces non-Euclidean geometries.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid's Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/05-euclids-geometry/index.qmd)

### Parallelogram {#parallelogram}

A quadrilateral with both pairs of opposite sides parallel; opposite sides are equal, opposite angles are equal, and the diagonals bisect each other.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/03-understanding-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)

### Parametric curve {#parametric-curve}

A curve traced by a moving point $(x(t), y(t))$ as a parameter $t$ varies — capturing both the *trail* of points and the *trip* of when each point is visited.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/15-parametric-equations/index.qmd)

### Parametric differentiation {#parametric-differentiation}

For curves given by $x = f(t),\ y = g(t)$, the slope is $dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5 — Differentiability and Rules of Differentiation](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)

### Partial derivative {#partial-derivative}

The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one variable, holding the others fixed; written $\partial f / \partial x_i$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 5 — Partial Derivatives and the Gradient](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/05-partial-derivatives/index.qmd)

### Partial differential equation {#partial-differential-equation}

An equation relating a function of several variables to its partial derivatives. Examples: heat, wave, Laplace, Schrödinger.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Partial fractions {#partial-fractions}

A technique for splitting a proper rational function $P(x)/Q(x)$ into a sum of simpler fractions whose denominators are the irreducible factors of $Q(x)$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 7 — Integration Techniques](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/07b-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Pascal's identity {#pascals-identity}

The recurrence $\binom{n}{r} + \binom{n}{r-1} = \binom{n+1}{r}$. Combinatorially: to choose $r$ from $n+1$, either include the new element (then choose $r-1$ from the original $n$) or exclude it (choose $r$ from $n$). This is the rule that builds Pascal's triangle row by row.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 6 — Permutations and Combinations](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/06-permutations-combinations/index.qmd)

### Pascal's triangle {#pascals-triangle}

The triangular array whose $n$-th row holds the binomial coefficients $\binom{n}{0}, \binom{n}{1}, \ldots, \binom{n}{n}$; each interior entry is the sum of the two entries diagonally above it. The row sums to $2^n$ and gives the coefficients in the expansion of $(a+b)^n$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 7 — Binomial Theorem](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/07-binomial-theorem/index.qmd)

### PDF (probability density function) {#pdf}

For a continuous random variable, the function whose area under the curve between $a$ and $b$ gives the probability of falling in that interval. Not a probability itself — a probability *density*.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 8 — Continuous Distributions and the Normal](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/08-continuous-distributions/index.qmd)

### Percent {#percent}

A ratio out of 100. The symbol $r\%$ stands for $\tfrac{r}{100}$, and "$r\%$ of $x$" is $\tfrac{r}{100} \cdot x$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/07-comparing-quantities/index.qmd)

### Perfect cube {#perfect-cube}

An integer of the form $n^3$ for some integer $n$. Equivalently, every prime in its factorisation appears with an exponent that is a multiple of three.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 6 — Cubes and Cube Roots](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/06-cubes-and-cube-roots/index.qmd)

### Perfect square {#perfect-square}

A whole number of the form $n^2$ for some whole $n$. Equivalently, every prime in its factorisation appears an even number of times.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 5 — Squares and Square Roots](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/05-squares-and-square-roots/index.qmd)

### Perimeter {#perimeter}

The total distance around the boundary of a flat shape, measured in length units (m, cm).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

### Permutation {#permutation}

An arrangement of $r$ items chosen from $n$ where order matters: $P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)!$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 11 — Combinatorics — Counting](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/11-combinatorics-counting/index.qmd)

### Perpendicular bisector {#perpendicular-bisector}

The line through the midpoint of a segment that meets it at a right angle; also the locus of points equidistant from the segment's two endpoints.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book I — Chapter 3, Perpendiculars and Obliques](lessons/tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/03-perpendiculars/index.qmd)

### Pi {#pi}

The ratio of any circle's circumference to its diameter — a constant $\approx 3.14159$ that is irrational and transcendental. CBSE board papers conventionally use $\pi = 22/7$.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/11-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)

### Pie chart {#pie-chart}

A circle divided into sectors whose central angles are proportional to category counts: angle $= (\text{count} / \text{total}) \times 360°$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 4 — Data Handling](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/04-data-handling/index.qmd)

### Place value {#place-value}

The rule that the value of a digit depends on which column it sits in. In our base-10 system, each step left multiplies the value by ten.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)

### Platonic solid {#platonic-solid}

A convex polyhedron whose faces are all congruent regular polygons and where the same number of faces meets at every vertex. Exactly five exist: tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, icosahedron.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 5, Regular polyhedra](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/05-regular-polyhedra/index.qmd)

### PMF (probability mass function) {#pmf}

A function that assigns each value of a discrete random variable its probability. The probabilities must sum to 1.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 15 — Discrete Probability Distributions](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/15-discrete-distributions/index.qmd)

### Point {#point}

A position with no size — the smallest object in geometry.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Polar form {#polar-form}

Writing a complex number as $z = re^{i\theta}$ where $r = |z|$ and $\theta = \arg(z)$. Multiplication then becomes "scale by $r$, rotate by $\theta$."

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 4 — Complex Numbers](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Policy evaluation {#policy-evaluation}

Computing the value function $v_\pi$ of a fixed policy $\pi$, either by closed-form $v_\pi = (I - \gamma P_\pi)^{-1} r_\pi$ or by iterating the linear operator $T^\pi$ to convergence (also a $\gamma$-contraction).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 14, Policy Iteration](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/14-policy-iteration/index.qmd)

### Policy gradient theorem {#policy-gradient-theorem}

The identity $\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(s,a) \sim \pi_\theta}[\nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a \mid s)\, q_{\pi_\theta}(s,a)]$. Lets the gradient of expected return be estimated from samples without knowing the dynamics.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 35, The Policy Gradient Theorem](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/35-policy-gradient-theorem/index.qmd)

### Policy improvement {#policy-improvement}

Constructing a new policy $\pi' = \arg\max_a [r + \gamma \sum P\,v_\pi]$ from the value function of an old policy $\pi$. The policy improvement theorem guarantees $v_{\pi'} \ge v_\pi$ pointwise.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 14, Policy Iteration](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/14-policy-iteration/index.qmd)

### Policy iteration {#policy-iteration}

The algorithm that alternates exact policy evaluation $v_{\pi_k} = (I - \gamma P_{\pi_k})^{-1} r_{\pi_k}$ with greedy improvement $\pi_{k+1} = \arg\max_a [r + \gamma \sum P\,v_{\pi_k}]$. Converges to $\pi^*$ in finitely many iterations on finite MDPs (Howard 1960).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 14, Policy Iteration](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/14-policy-iteration/index.qmd)

### Policy (RL) {#policy-rl}

A conditional distribution $\pi(a \mid s)$ over actions given the current state. Deterministic policies put probability 1 on a single action; combining a policy with an MDP collapses it into a Markov chain.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 03, Policies as Conditional Distributions](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/03-policy/index.qmd)

### Polygon {#polygon}

A closed flat shape with straight sides — triangle, quadrilateral, pentagon, and so on.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Polynomial {#polynomial}

A sum of terms, each of the form (a number) × (a non-negative whole-number power of $x$). Example: $3x^2 - 4x + 7$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 11 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/11-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Position vector {#position-vector}

The vector from the origin to a point $P$, written $\vec{OP}$. In coordinates, $\vec{OP} = x\hat\imath + y\hat\jmath + z\hat k$ for $P = (x, y, z)$ — same numbers as the point's coordinates, now interpreted as the components of an arrow anchored at $O$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 10a — Vector Algebra](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/10a-vector-algebra/index.qmd)

### Post-quantum cryptography {#post-quantum-cryptography}

Cryptographic schemes designed to remain secure against attackers equipped with a large-scale quantum computer. RSA, ECC, and Diffie-Hellman are all broken by Shor's algorithm; PQC candidates rely on problems (lattice problems, code-based, hash-based, multivariate, isogeny) for which no polynomial-time quantum algorithm is known. NIST standardised CRYSTALS-Kyber (FIPS 203) and CRYSTALS-Dilithium (FIPS 204) in 2024.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 14 — Lattice-Based Post-Quantum Cryptography](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/14-lattice-crypto/index.qmd)

### Postulate {#postulate}

A geometrical statement accepted without proof as the foundation of an axiomatic geometry. Euclid stated five.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 5 — Introduction to Euclid's Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/05-euclids-geometry/index.qmd)

### Potential function {#potential-function}

A scalar function $f$ whose gradient equals a given vector field, $\mathbf{F} = \nabla f$; such fields are called *conservative* and have zero curl.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Power rule {#power-rule}

$\dfrac{d}{dx} x^n = n x^{n-1}$ for any real $n$ — the workhorse derivative formula.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Prime {#prime}

A whole number $\ge 2$ with exactly two divisors: 1 and itself. The first few are 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Prime factorisation {#prime-factorisation}

The unique way of writing a whole number $\ge 2$ as a product of primes. The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic guarantees uniqueness.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Principal argument {#principal-argument}

The unique value of $\arg(z)$ for a non-zero complex number $z$ that lies in $(-\pi, \pi]$, written $\operatorname{Arg}(z)$. Found by computing the reference angle $\arctan(|b|/|a|)$ and adjusting by quadrant.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 4 — Complex Numbers and Quadratic Equations](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)

### Principal value branch (inverse trig) {#principal-branch}

The restricted domain on which a trigonometric function is one-one and onto its full range, making it invertible. For $\sin$: $[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$; for $\cos$: $[0, \pi]$; for $\tan$: $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 2 — Inverse Trigonometric Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/02-inverse-trig-functions/index.qmd)

### Prism {#prism}

A polyhedron with two parallel congruent polygonal bases joined by parallelograms (rectangles in the right case). Volume = base area × height.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/02-polyhedra/index.qmd)

### Probability {#probability}

A number between 0 and 1 measuring how likely an event is. For equally likely outcomes, $P(\text{event}) = \text{favourable outcomes} / \text{total outcomes}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd)

### Product rule {#product-rule}

$(fg)' = f'g + fg'$ — never $f'g'$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

### Projected Bellman operator {#projected-bellman-operator}

The composition $\Pi_\mu T_\pi$: project onto the feature span $\mathcal{F}$ under the $\mu$-weighted norm, then apply the Bellman operator $T_\pi$. On-policy ($\mu = \mu_\pi$) it is a $\gamma$-contraction in $\|\cdot\|_\mu$ with a unique TD fixed point; off-policy ($\mu = \mu_b$) the contraction can break and the iteration can diverge — one face of the deadly triad.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 32, The Projected Bellman Operator and MSPBE](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/32-projected-bellman-operator/index.qmd)

### Proof {#proof}

A finite chain of justified steps deriving a conclusion from accepted assumptions; the standard certificate of mathematical truth.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Proportion {#proportion}

A statement that two ratios are equal; cross-multiplication finds the missing piece.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### p-value {#p-value}

The probability, assuming the null hypothesis is true, of observing data at least as extreme as what was actually seen.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 10 — Statistical Inference](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/10-statistical-inference/index.qmd)

### Pyramid {#pyramid}

A polyhedron with one polygonal base and one apex; lateral faces are triangles. Volume = $\tfrac{1}{3}$ × base area × height — the 3D cousin of the half-base-times-height triangle.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 2, Polyhedra](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/02-polyhedra/index.qmd)

### Pythagorean identities {#pythagorean-identities}

The three trigonometric identities — $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$, $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$, $1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta$ — that follow from $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ by dividing by $c^2$, $b^2$, $a^2$ respectively.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 8 — Trigonometric Identities](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/08b-trigonometric-identities/index.qmd)

### Pythagorean theorem {#pythagorean-theorem}

In a right-angled triangle with legs $a, b$ and hypotenuse $c$, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$. Provable by similarity, by area-rearrangement, or by coordinate algebra.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 10 — The Pythagorean Theorem](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/10-pythagorean-theorem/index.qmd)

## Q

### Q-learning {#q-learning}

Off-policy TD(0) for $q^*$: $q(s,a) \leftarrow q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} q(s',a') - q(s,a)]$; converges to optimal action values regardless of the data-generating policy.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 26, Q-Learning](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/26-q-learning/index.qmd)

### Quadrant {#quadrant}

One of the four regions into which the two coordinate axes divide the plane, numbered I–IV counter-clockwise from the upper right.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 3 — Coordinate Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/03-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)

### Quadratic equation {#quadratic-equation}

An equation of the form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$, with $a \ne 0$. Has 2, 1, or 0 real solutions depending on the discriminant.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

### Quadrilateral {#quadrilateral}

A closed plane figure bounded by four line segments. Its four interior angles always sum to $360°$.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 3 — Understanding Quadrilaterals](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/03-understanding-quadrilaterals/index.qmd)

### Quaternion {#quaternion}

A 4-real-number $a + bi + cj + dk$ obeying Hamilton's relations $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1$; the unit quaternions form $S^3$ and double-cover the rotation group $SO(3)$, giving the singularity-free representation of 3D rotation used in animation, robotics, and spacecraft attitude control.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 8 — Quaternions and 3D Rotations](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/08-quaternions/index.qmd)

### Quotient {#quotient}

The result of a division — each fair share when the total is split.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Quotient rule {#quotient-rule}

$(f/g)' = (f'g - fg')/g^2$ — "low d-high minus high d-low, all over low squared."

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 4 — Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/04-differentiation-rules/index.qmd)

## R

### Radian {#radian}

The angle subtended at the centre of a circle by an arc whose length equals the radius. $180° = \pi$ radians; calculus formulas for trig functions only work nicely in radians.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)

### Radicand {#radicand}

The expression sitting under a radical sign. For an even-index root over the reals, the radicand must be non-negative.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/02-relations-and-functions/index.qmd)

### Range {#range}

The largest value in a dataset minus the smallest — a simple measure of spread.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 14 — Data and the Average](lessons/tier-1-foundations/14-data-and-average/index.qmd)

### Rate {#rate}

A ratio of two quantities with different units, like km per litre or rupees per kg.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### Ratio {#ratio}

A comparison of two quantities by division — the relationship that survives when both quantities are scaled by the same factor.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 9 — Ratio and Proportion](lessons/tier-1-foundations/09-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

### Rational number {#rational-number}

A number that can be written $\tfrac{p}{q}$ with $p, q$ integers and $q \ne 0$. Many fractions name the same rational; between any two distinct rationals, infinitely many more lie.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 1 — Rational Numbers](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/01-rational-numbers/index.qmd)

### Rational-root theorem {#rational-root-theorem}

For a polynomial with integer coefficients, every rational root $p/q$ (in lowest terms) satisfies $p \mid a_0$ (constant term) and $q \mid a_n$ (leading coefficient). The standard first attack on cubics and quartics with integer coefficients.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 2, Higher-degree equations](lessons/tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/02-higher-degree-equations/index.qmd)

### Real number {#real-number}

Any number on the number line — every rational together with every irrational. Reals fill the line completely, leaving no gaps between neighbours.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/01-number-systems/index.qmd)

### Reciprocal {#reciprocal}

The fraction obtained by flipping numerator and denominator. Dividing by a fraction is the same as multiplying by its reciprocal.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 7 — Fractions](lessons/tier-1-foundations/07-fractions-as-parts/index.qmd)

### Regular polygon {#regular-polygon}

A convex polygon with all sides equal AND all interior angles equal. Inscribes in and circumscribes about a common circle; interior angle is $(n-2) \cdot 180° / n$.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book I — Chapter 8, Regular polygons and circumference](lessons/tier-kiselev-3-planimetry/08-regular-polygons/index.qmd)

### REINFORCE {#reinforce}

Stochastic gradient ascent on $J(\theta)$ that uses the observed Monte Carlo return $G_t$ as an unbiased single-sample estimator of $q_{\pi_\theta}(s_t, a_t)$ inside the policy gradient theorem (Williams 1992).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 36, REINFORCE](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/36-reinforce/index.qmd)

### Related rates {#related-rates}

A class of problems where two quantities are linked by an equation and you find one rate of change from the other by differentiating with respect to time.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 7 — Related Rates](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/07-related-rates/index.qmd)

### Relation {#relation}

Any subset of the Cartesian product $A \times B$ — i.e. any rule that decides which ordered pairs $(a, b)$ "belong together." Functions are the special relations where every input has exactly one output.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 2 — Relations and Functions](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/02-relations-and-functions/index.qmd)

### Remainder {#remainder}

What's left over after a division when the total doesn't split exactly. The defining equation: total = divisor × quotient + remainder.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 6 — Division as Fair Share](lessons/tier-1-foundations/06-divide-fair-share/index.qmd)

### Remainder theorem {#remainder-theorem}

When a polynomial $p(x)$ is divided by $(x - a)$, the remainder equals $p(a)$. Plugging in a value and dividing by a linear factor are the same act.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 2 — Polynomials](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/02-polynomials/index.qmd)

### Removable discontinuity {#removable-discontinuity}

A point where a function fails to be continuous only because of a single missing or wrong value — patchable by redefining $f(a)$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 2 — Continuity](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/02-continuity/index.qmd)

### Repeating decimal {#repeating-decimal}

A decimal whose digits cycle forever in a fixed pattern, written with a bar over the repeating block (e.g. $0.\overline{3}$). Every fraction is either terminating or repeating.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Replay buffer {#replay-buffer}

A circular buffer of past transitions $(s, a, r, s')$ from which mini-batches are sampled uniformly to train a value or actor-critic network. Decorrelates samples and reuses each transition many times.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 31, Deep Q-Network](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)

### Residue theorem {#residue-theorem}

$\oint_C f(z)\, dz = 2\pi i \sum_k \mathrm{Res}(f; a_k)$, summed over singularities inside $C$. Lets contour integrals (and many real integrals) be computed from local data at singular points.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 5 — Complex Functions](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/05-complex-functions/index.qmd)

### Riemann sum {#riemann-sum}

A finite sum $\sum f(x_i^*) \Delta x$ approximating area under a curve by rectangles; converges to the definite integral as $\Delta x \to 0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 8 — The Definite Integral](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/08-definite-integral/index.qmd)

### Right angle {#right-angle}

A quarter-turn — exactly $90°$. The corner of a book, a window, or a square.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Right-hand rule {#right-hand-rule}

A mnemonic for fixing the direction of $\vec a \times \vec b$: point the fingers of your right hand from $\vec a$ to $\vec b$ through the smaller angle and curl them; the thumb gives the direction of the cross product. Equivalently, $(\hat\imath, \hat\jmath, \hat k)$ form a right-handed triple — bend your hand from $\hat\imath$ to $\hat\jmath$ and the thumb points along $\hat k$.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 10b — Dot Product, Cross Product, and Applications](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/10b-dot-cross-product-applications/index.qmd)

### Ring {#ring}

A set with two operations (add and multiply): an abelian group under $+$, an associative $\cdot$, tied together by distributivity. Generalises the integers.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 2 — Rings and Fields](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/02-rings-fields/index.qmd)

### Robbins-Monro algorithm {#robbins-monro-algorithm}

Stochastic root-finder $w_{k+1} = w_k - \alpha_k y_k$ for solving $g(w) = 0$ from noisy samples $y_k = g(w_k) + \eta_k$; converges almost surely when $\sum\alpha_k = \infty$ and $\sum\alpha_k^2 < \infty$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 20, Robbins-Monro](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/20-robbins-monro/index.qmd)

### RSA {#rsa}

A widely used encryption scheme whose security rests on the difficulty of factoring a product of two large primes.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 15 — Divisibility and Primes](lessons/tier-1-foundations/15-divisibility-and-primes/index.qmd)

### Rule of three {#rule-of-three}

Given three of four quantities in a proportion $a : b = c : d$, find the fourth by cross-multiplication. The workhorse algorithm of applied arithmetic for two millennia, predating symbolic algebra.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Arithmetic — Chapter 5, Ratio and proportion](lessons/tier-kiselev-0-arithmetic/05-ratio-proportion/index.qmd)

## S

### Sample space {#sample-space}

The set of every possible outcome of a random experiment. Every probability is computed relative to it.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 13 — Basic Probability](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/13-basic-probability/index.qmd)

### Sarsa {#sarsa}

On-policy TD(0) for action values: $q(s,a) \leftarrow q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma q(s',a') - q(s,a)]$ with $a' \sim \pi$; named after the data tuple $(s, a, r, s', a')$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 24, Sarsa](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/24-sarsa/index.qmd)

### Scalar triple product {#scalar-triple-product}

The number $[\vec a\ \vec b\ \vec c] = \vec a \cdot (\vec b \times \vec c)$ — equivalently, the determinant whose rows are the components of $\vec a, \vec b, \vec c$. Geometrically it is the signed volume of the parallelepiped they span; it vanishes exactly when the three vectors are coplanar.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 10b — Dot Product, Cross Product, and Applications](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/10b-dot-cross-product-applications/index.qmd)

### Scientific notation {#scientific-notation}

A way of writing any number as $a \times 10^n$ with $1 \le a < 10$ and $n$ an integer — the shape and size kept separate.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 8 — Scientific Notation](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/08-scientific-notation/index.qmd)

### Secant line {#secant-line}

A line passing through two points on a curve; its slope is the average rate of change between them.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Second derivative {#second-derivative}

The derivative of $f'$ — written $f''(x)$ or $\dfrac{d^2y}{dx^2}$. Geometrically it measures how the slope is changing: convexity, acceleration, curvature.

**First named in:** [Class 12, Chapter 5b — Differentiability and Differentiation Rules](lessons/tier-cbse-12-class-12/05b-differentiability-and-rules/index.qmd)

### Second derivative test {#second-derivative-test}

At a critical point $c$: $f''(c) < 0$ → local max; $f''(c) > 0$ → local min; $f''(c) = 0$ → inconclusive.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 6 — Optimisation](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/06-optimization/index.qmd)

### Section formula {#section-formula}

The coordinates of a point dividing the segment from $(x_1, y_1)$ to $(x_2, y_2)$ in ratio $m{:}n$ are $\left(\dfrac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n}, \dfrac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n}\right)$.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 7 — Coordinate Geometry](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/07-coordinate-geometry/index.qmd)

### Sector {#sector}

The pie-slice region of a circle bounded by two radii and the arc between them. A sector with central angle $\theta$ has area $(\theta/360°)\pi r^2$ and arc length $(\theta/360°)\,2\pi r$.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/11-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)

### Segment (of a circle) {#segment-circle}

The 2D region of a circle bounded by a chord and one of the two arcs the chord cuts off. Minor-segment area = sector area $-$ triangle area.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 11 — Areas Related to Circles](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/11-areas-related-to-circles/index.qmd)

### Semi-gradient TD {#semi-gradient-td}

A TD update for a parameterised value function that differentiates the prediction but treats the bootstrapped target as constant in $w$, even though it depends on $w$. Provably convergent for linear FA on-policy (Tsitsiklis–Van Roy 1997).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 29, TD with Function Approximation](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/29-td-with-function-approximation/index.qmd)

### Semi-perimeter {#semi-perimeter}

Half the perimeter of a polygon — the "$s$" in Heron's formula.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 10 — Heron's Formula](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/10-herons-formula/index.qmd)

### Separation of variables {#separation-of-variables}

The technique of seeking PDE solutions of the product form $u(x, t) = X(x)\,T(t)$, which (for linear PDEs on nice domains) reduces a PDE to two ODEs joined by a shared separation constant.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

### Sequence {#sequence}

An ordered list of numbers, written $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$. Each position has its own term.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 15 — Sequences (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/15-sequences-intro/index.qmd)

### Series {#series}

The running sum of a sequence — what you get by adding the first term, then the first two, then the first three, and so on. A series can converge to a finite number or diverge to infinity.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 4 — Sequences and Series](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/04-sequences-series/index.qmd)

### Set {#set}

A collection of distinct objects, with operations union, intersection, difference, and complement; the foundational data type of modern mathematics.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 11 — Sets, Logic, and Proof Techniques](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/11-sets-logic-proofs/index.qmd)

### Sign chart {#sign-chart}

A diagram of the real number line with the roots of a factored polynomial (or the zeros of numerator and denominator of a rational expression) marked, recording the sign of the expression in each resulting interval. Sign flips at odd-multiplicity roots, holds at even-multiplicity roots; the standard tool for solving polynomial and rational inequalities.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Algebra Part II — Chapter 3, Higher-degree inequalities](lessons/tier-kiselev-2-algebra-2/03-higher-degree-inequalities/index.qmd)

### Similar figures {#similar-figures}

Two figures that have the same shape but possibly different size — corresponding angles equal, corresponding sides in the same ratio.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)

### Simple interest {#simple-interest}

Interest computed only on the original principal: $\text{SI} = \tfrac{P \cdot R \cdot T}{100}$. Each year adds the same amount.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 7 — Comparing Quantities](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/07-comparing-quantities/index.qmd)

### Skew lines {#skew-lines}

Two lines in space that neither meet nor are parallel — the third configuration that exists in 3D but not in the plane.

**First named in:** [Kiselev Geometry Book II — Chapter 1, Lines and planes](lessons/tier-kiselev-4-stereometry/01-lines-and-planes/index.qmd)

### Slope {#slope}

For a straight line, the change in $y$ per unit change in $x$ — its rise over run. Captures how steeply the line climbs.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

### Smooth manifold {#smooth-manifold}

A topological space that locally looks like $\mathbb{R}^n$, with overlapping coordinate charts whose transition maps are infinitely differentiable; the natural setting for differential calculus on curved spaces.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/07-differential-geometry/index.qmd)

### Softmax policy {#softmax-policy}

A parameterised discrete policy $\pi(a \mid s; \theta) = \exp(h(s,a;\theta)) / \sum_{a'}\exp(h(s,a';\theta))$, where $h$ is a learnable preference. Smoothly interpolates between uniform random and greedy.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 34, Parameterising Policies](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/34-policy-parameterization/index.qmd)

### Solution of an equation {#solution-of-an-equation}

A value of the unknown that, when substituted back in, makes both sides of the equation equal.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 2 — Linear Equations in One Variable](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)

### Spectral theorem {#spectral-theorem}

Every real symmetric matrix factors as $A = QDQ^T$ with $Q$ orthogonal and $D$ diagonal of real eigenvalues; an orthonormal eigenbasis always exists.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### Sphere {#sphere}

The set of points in 3D space at a fixed distance (radius $r$) from a centre. Surface area $4\pi r^2$, volume $\tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 11 — Surface Areas and Volumes](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/11-surface-areas-and-volumes/index.qmd)

### Square-cube law {#square-cube-law}

When you scale a figure by factor $k$, lengths multiply by $k$, areas by $k^2$, and volumes by $k^3$. The reason a giant ant could not stand and an elephant has thick legs.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 13 — Similarity and Scale](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/13-similarity-scale/index.qmd)

### Square root {#square-root}

The non-negative number that, multiplied by itself, gives a chosen non-negative number. The square root of 9 is 3 because $3 \times 3 = 9$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 9 — Square Roots and Irrationals](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/09-square-roots-irrationals/index.qmd)

### Standard deviation {#standard-deviation}

The square root of variance, $\sigma = \sqrt{\text{Var}(X)}$ — spread in the original units of $X$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### Standard error {#standard-error}

The standard deviation of a sampling distribution; for a sample mean it is $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### State-transition kernel {#state-transition-kernel}

The conditional distribution $P(s' \mid s, a)$ specifying the probability of arriving in next state $s'$ when action $a$ is taken in state $s$. Each row sums to 1.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 02, State Transitions and the Markov Property](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/02-state-transitions/index.qmd)

### State value {#state-value}

$v_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi[G_t \mid s_t = s]$ — the expected discounted return starting from state $s$ and following policy $\pi$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 05, State Values: Expected Return](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/05-state-values/index.qmd)

### Substitution method {#substitution-method}

A way to solve a system of equations: isolate one variable in one equation, then plug that expression into the other equation.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

### Subtraction {#subtraction}

Taking one number away from another — pictured on the number line as a hop to the left.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 4 — Addition and Subtraction](lessons/tier-1-foundations/04-add-subtract/index.qmd)

### Surd {#surd}

An irrational root, especially a square or cube root that cannot be simplified to a rational. For example, $\sqrt{2}$ and $\sqrt[3]{5}$ are surds; $\sqrt{4} = 2$ is not.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 1 — Number Systems](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/01-number-systems/index.qmd)

### SVD {#svd}

Singular Value Decomposition: every matrix factors as $A = U\Sigma V^T$ with $U, V$ orthogonal and $\Sigma$ diagonal non-negative; the universal generalisation of the spectral theorem.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 15 — The Spectral Theorem](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/15-spectral-theorem/index.qmd)

### System of linear equations {#system-of-linear-equations}

Two or more linear equations considered together. A solution is a set of values that satisfies every equation at once.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 6 — Systems of Linear Equations](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/06-systems-linear-equations/index.qmd)

## T

### Tangent line {#tangent-line}

The line that just touches a curve at a single point and matches its slope there — the limit of secant lines.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 3 — The Derivative](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/03-derivative/index.qmd)

### Tangent space {#tangent-space}

At a point $p$ on a smooth manifold $M$, the vector space $T_pM$ of velocities of curves through $p$; the linear approximation that lets you do calculus and host the Riemannian metric.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 7 — Differential Geometry](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/07-differential-geometry/index.qmd)

### Tangent (to a circle) {#tangent-circle}

A line that meets a circle at exactly one point — the *point of contact*. The tangent at the point of contact is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point.

**First named in:** [Class 10, Chapter 10 — Circles and Tangents](lessons/tier-cbse-10-class-10/10-circles-tangents/index.qmd)

### Target network {#target-network}

A frozen (or slowly-updated) copy of a Q-network used to compute bootstrap targets, so that the regression target does not move while the main parameters are updated. Hard sync every $C$ steps (DQN) or soft update via exponential moving average (DDPG).

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 31, Deep Q-Network](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/31-deep-q-network/index.qmd)

### TD error {#td-error}

The quantity $\delta_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma v(s_{t+1}) - v(s_t)$: the gap between the bootstrapped target and the current estimate; drives every TD update.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 23, TD(0)](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/23-td-state-values/index.qmd)

### TD(0) {#td-zero}

The one-step temporal-difference update $v(s) \leftarrow v(s) + \alpha[r + \gamma v(s') - v(s)]$; estimates $v_\pi$ from sampled transitions without waiting for episode end.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 23, TD(0)](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/23-td-state-values/index.qmd)

### TDC {#tdc}

"TD with gradient correction" — the lowest-variance member of the [gradient-TD family](#gradient-td). Per-sample updates: $w \leftarrow w + \alpha[\delta\,\phi - \gamma\,\phi'(\phi^\top u)]$ and $u \leftarrow u + \beta(\delta - \phi^\top u)\,\phi$ with $\beta > \alpha$. Reduces to semi-gradient TD when $u = 0$; same fixed point as GTD2 but typically faster.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 33, Gradient-TD: GTD, GTD2, and TDC](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/33-gradient-td/index.qmd)

### Terminating decimal {#terminating-decimal}

A decimal that ends after finitely many digits — equivalently, a fraction whose reduced denominator's only prime factors are 2 and 5.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 8 — Decimals](lessons/tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)

### Topology {#topology}

A choice of which subsets of a space count as "open," closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections; that one choice is enough to define continuity, convergence, compactness, and connectedness without any reference to distance.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 2 — Topology](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/02-topology/index.qmd)

### Trajectory {#trajectory}

A sequence $(s_0, a_0, r_1, s_1, a_1, r_2, \ldots)$ generated by an MDP under a policy — the "tape" of one rollout.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 04, Trajectories, Returns, and the Discount](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/04-trajectories-returns-episodes/index.qmd)

### Transversal {#transversal}

A line that crosses two or more other lines. When the crossed lines are parallel, a transversal generates equal corresponding and alternate-interior angle pairs and supplementary co-interior pairs.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd)

### Trapezium {#trapezium}

A four-sided figure with exactly one pair of parallel sides; area $\tfrac{1}{2}(a + b) h$ where $a, b$ are the parallel sides and $h$ the perpendicular distance between them.

**First named in:** [Class 8, Chapter 9 — Mensuration](lessons/tier-cbse-08-class-8/09-mensuration/index.qmd)

### Tree {#tree}

A connected graph with no cycles; on $n$ vertices it has exactly $n-1$ edges.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 12 — Graphs and Their Algorithms](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/12-graphs-algorithms/index.qmd)

### Triangle {#triangle}

A polygon with three straight sides; its three interior angles always sum to $180°$.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 12 — Shapes and Angles](lessons/tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)

### Trigonometric equation {#trigonometric-equation}

An equation involving trigonometric functions of an unknown angle. Because trig functions are periodic, solutions come in infinite families, parametrised by an integer $n$.

**First named in:** [Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Equations](lessons/tier-cbse-11-class-11/03c-trig-equations/index.qmd)

### Trigonometric function {#trigonometric-function}

A function (sine, cosine, tangent, and their reciprocals) that converts an angle into a number — built from the coordinates of a point travelling around the unit circle.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 7 — Trig Ratios](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/07-trig-ratios/index.qmd)
### Truncated policy iteration {#truncated-policy-iteration}

A family of algorithms parameterised by $K$ that performs $K$ Bellman evaluation steps per greedy improvement. Recovers value iteration ($K = 1$) and policy iteration ($K = \infty$) as endpoints; any $K \ge 1$ converges.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 15, Truncated Policy Iteration](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/15-truncated-policy-iteration/index.qmd)


## U

### Uncertainty principle {#uncertainty-principle}

The Fourier-pair inequality $\sigma_x \sigma_k \ge 1/2$: a signal cannot be sharply localised in both time and frequency. In quantum mechanics this becomes Heisenberg's $\sigma_x \sigma_p \ge \hbar/2$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 13 — The Fourier Transform](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/13-fourier-transform/index.qmd)

### u-substitution {#u-substitution}

An integration trick that runs the chain rule backwards: rename an inner expression as $u$, replace $dx$ with $du$, and the integral often simplifies to a standard form.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 10 — Techniques of Integration](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/10-integration-techniques/index.qmd)

### Unit circle {#unit-circle}

The circle of radius 1 centred at the origin; the geometric foundation of the trigonometric functions.

**First named in:** [Tier 3, Lesson 8 — The Unit Circle](lessons/tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)

### Unit vector {#unit-vector}

A vector of magnitude 1 — pure direction with the size stripped away. Found by dividing any non-zero vector by its magnitude.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

## V

### Value function approximation {#value-function-approximation}

Replacing the table $v(s)$ by a parameterised function $\hat v(s; w)$ with $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $d \ll |\mathcal{S}|$; trades exact representation for memory and built-in generalisation.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 28, From Table to Function](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/28-from-table-to-function/index.qmd)

### Value iteration {#value-iteration}

The algorithm $v_{k+1} \leftarrow T^* v_k$, repeatedly applying the Bellman optimality operator until convergence. Equivalent to Banach iteration on $T^*$; converges geometrically at rate $\gamma^k$.

**First named in:** [MFRL — Lesson 13, Value Iteration](lessons/tier-mfrl-rl-math/13-value-iteration/index.qmd)

### Variable {#variable}

A letter that stands for a number — sometimes one specific unknown, sometimes any number in a range.

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](lessons/tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)

### Variance {#variance}

The expected squared deviation of a random variable from its mean: $\text{Var}(X) = E[(X-\mu)^2]$. Measures spread in squared units.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 9 — Variance, Standard Deviation, and the Central Limit Theorem](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/09-variance-clt/index.qmd)

### VC dimension {#vc-dimension}

The largest number of points a hypothesis class can label every possible way (shatter); a finite VC dimension is exactly the condition for PAC learnability and controls the generalisation gap as $\sqrt{d/n}$.

**First named in:** [Tier 7, Lesson 4 — Statistical Learning Theory](lessons/tier-7-masters-tracks/04-statistical-learning-theory/index.qmd)

### Vector {#vector}

A quantity with both magnitude and direction, written as a tuple of coordinates such as $(x, y)$ in 2D or $(x, y, z)$ in 3D.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 13 — Vectors in 2D and 3D](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)

### Vector field {#vector-field}

A function that assigns a vector to every point of a region — a "sea of arrows" describing flow, force, or velocity.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 7 — Vector Fields, Divergence, Curl](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/07-vector-fields/index.qmd)

### Vector space {#vector-space}

A set on which addition and scalar multiplication are defined and satisfy the standard eight axioms — the abstract setting that unifies arrows, polynomials, and function spaces.

**First named in:** [Tier 5, Lesson 3 — Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis](lessons/tier-5-undergrad-core/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Velocity vector {#velocity-vector}

For a parametric curve $(x(t), y(t))$, the vector $(x'(t), y'(t))$ — pointing in the direction of motion, with length equal to the speed.

**First named in:** [Tier 4, Lesson 15 — Parametric Equations and Curves](lessons/tier-4-precalc-calculus/15-parametric-equations/index.qmd)

### Vertically opposite angles {#vertically-opposite-angles}

The pair of angles directly across from each other when two lines cross. They are always equal.

**First named in:** [Class 9, Chapter 6 — Lines and Angles](lessons/tier-cbse-09-class-9/06-lines-and-angles/index.qmd)

### Volume {#volume}

The size of the space inside a 3D shape, measured in cubic units (m³, cm³).

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 13 — Area, Perimeter, Volume](lessons/tier-1-foundations/13-area-perimeter-volume/index.qmd)

## W

### Wave equation {#wave-equation}

$u_{tt} = c^2\, u_{xx}$. Models vibrating strings, sound, light. d'Alembert's solution: $u(x,t) = f(x - ct) + g(x + ct)$ — left- and right-moving waves at speed $c$.

**First named in:** [Tier 6, Lesson 7 — PDEs](lessons/tier-6-undergrad-advanced/07-pdes/index.qmd)

## X

*(No entries yet.)*

## Y

### y-intercept {#y-intercept}

The value of $y$ where a graph crosses the y-axis — equivalently, the value of $y$ when $x = 0$.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 5 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)

## Z

### Zero {#zero}

The number meaning "nothing of this". As a placeholder it keeps other digits in their proper columns; without it, "305" collapses into "35".

**First named in:** [Tier 1, Lesson 3 — Place Value](lessons/tier-1-foundations/03-place-value/index.qmd)

### Zero-product property {#zero-product-property}

If a product of two numbers is zero, then at least one of them must be zero. The hinge that turns a factored quadratic into its roots.

**First named in:** [Tier 2, Lesson 14 — Quadratic Equations (Intro)](lessons/tier-2-prealgebra/14-quadratic-equations-intro/index.qmd)

---

## MML track additions

The following entries are first-named in the **Mathematics for Machine Learning (mml)** track, which walks Deisenroth, Faisal & Ong end-to-end. Many of these terms are also referenced in the synthesis tiers; the mml lesson gives the ML-flavoured framing.

### Affine subspace {#affine-subspace}

A subspace translated by a fixed point; geometrically a line, plane, or higher-dimensional flat that does *not* necessarily pass through the origin.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 06, Affine Spaces and Hyperplanes](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/06-affine-spaces/index.qmd)

### Automatic differentiation (autodiff) {#automatic-differentiation}

Algorithmic computation of exact derivatives of programs, in two flavours: *forward-mode* (best for $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}^M$) and *reverse-mode* (best for $\mathbb{R}^D \to \mathbb{R}$ — the basis of backprop).

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 16, Backpropagation and Automatic Differentiation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/16-backprop-autodiff/index.qmd)

### Backpropagation {#backpropagation}

Reverse-mode automatic differentiation applied to neural networks: forward pass computes outputs; backward pass propagates gradient through the graph in reverse. Cost: ~2× forward, regardless of parameter count.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 16, Backpropagation and Automatic Differentiation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/16-backprop-autodiff/index.qmd)

### Bayesian linear regression {#bayesian-linear-regression}

Linear regression where the weights have a Gaussian prior; the posterior is Gaussian by conjugacy, the MAP estimate is ridge regression, and the predictive distribution includes both parameter uncertainty and observation noise.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 25, Bayesian Linear Regression](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/25-bayesian-linear-regression/index.qmd)

### Cholesky decomposition {#cholesky-decomposition}

For an SPD (symmetric positive-definite) matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$, the unique factorisation $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{L}\boldsymbol{L}^\top$ with $\boldsymbol{L}$ lower-triangular and positive-diagonal. The "matrix square root" — twice as fast as LU and the canonical tool for solving / sampling / log-det of SPD matrices.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 11, Cholesky Decomposition](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/11-cholesky/index.qmd)

### Conditional independence {#conditional-independence}

$A \perp\!\!\!\perp B \mid C$ — observing $C$ makes $A$ and $B$ statistically independent. The fundamental relation encoded by directed graphical models.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 23, Directed Graphical Models](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/23-graphical-models-model-selection/index.qmd)

### Conjugate prior {#conjugate-prior}

A prior $p(\theta)$ is conjugate to a likelihood $p(D \mid \theta)$ if the posterior $p(\theta \mid D)$ is in the same family as the prior. Makes Bayes update reduce to parameter arithmetic.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 19, Conjugacy and the Exponential Family](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/19-conjugacy-change-of-variables/index.qmd)

### Cosine similarity {#cosine-similarity}

$\boldsymbol{u}^\top \boldsymbol{v} / (\lVert \boldsymbol{u}\rVert \lVert \boldsymbol{v}\rVert)$ — the cosine of the angle between two vectors; scale-free, used everywhere in embedding-based search.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 07, Norms, Inner Products, Lengths, Angles](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/07-norms-inner-products/index.qmd)

### Cross-validation {#cross-validation}

Partition data into $K$ folds; for each, train on the rest and evaluate on the held-out. Average across folds gives an estimate of true generalisation error. The modern default for model selection.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 22, Data, Models, ERM](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/22-models-and-erm/index.qmd)

### Design matrix {#design-matrix}

$\boldsymbol{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times D}$ — rows are examples, columns are features. The data structure every supervised-learning algorithm starts from.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 02, Matrices](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/02-matrices/index.qmd)

### Directed graphical model {#directed-graphical-model}

A DAG with random variables as nodes; the joint distribution factorises as $p(\boldsymbol{x}) = \prod_i p(x_i \mid \mathrm{Pa}(x_i))$. Missing edges encode conditional independences.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 23, Directed Graphical Models](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/23-graphical-models-model-selection/index.qmd)

### Eigendecomposition {#eigendecomposition}

A square matrix $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{S}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\boldsymbol{S}^{-1}$ written as basis (columns of $\boldsymbol{S}$ are eigenvectors) times diagonal eigenvalue matrix times inverse basis. For symmetric matrices, $\boldsymbol{S}$ is orthogonal — the spectral theorem.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 12, Eigendecomposition and Diagonalisation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/12-eigendecomposition/index.qmd)

### ELBO (Evidence Lower BOund) {#elbo}

For any latent-variable model and any distribution $q(z)$: $\log p(\boldsymbol{x}) \ge \mathbb{E}_q[\log p(\boldsymbol{x}, z)] - \mathbb{E}_q[\log q(z)]$. The right-hand side is the ELBO, maximised at the true posterior. The foundation of EM and variational inference.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 30, The EM Algorithm](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/30-em-algorithm/index.qmd)

### EM algorithm (Expectation-Maximisation) {#em-algorithm}

Iterative algorithm for MLE in latent-variable models: E-step computes posterior over latents given current parameters; M-step re-fits parameters given the posterior. Monotone improvement of log-likelihood; converges to local maximum.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 30, The EM Algorithm](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/30-em-algorithm/index.qmd)

### Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM) {#erm}

Minimise the average loss on the training data: $\min \tfrac{1}{N} \sum_n L(y_n, f_{\boldsymbol{w}}(\boldsymbol{x}_n))$. The universal recipe of supervised learning.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 22, Data, Models, ERM](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/22-models-and-erm/index.qmd)

### Exponential family {#exponential-family}

A parametric family with density $p(x \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) = h(x) \exp(\boldsymbol{\theta}^\top \boldsymbol{T}(x) - A(\boldsymbol{\theta}))$. Sufficient statistics live in $\boldsymbol{T}$; most common distributions (Gaussian, Beta, Gamma, Poisson, Bernoulli) belong to this family.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 19, Conjugacy and the Exponential Family](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/19-conjugacy-change-of-variables/index.qmd)

### Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) {#gmm}

A density $p(\boldsymbol{x}) = \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\mu}_k, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_k)$ — weighted sum of $K$ Gaussians. Used for soft clustering and density estimation; fitted by EM.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 29, Gaussian Mixture Models](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/29-gmm/index.qmd)

### Hessian {#hessian}

The matrix $\boldsymbol{H}_{ij} = \partial^2 f / \partial x_i \partial x_j$ of second partial derivatives of a scalar function. Symmetric (under continuity). Its eigenvalues at a critical point classify min / max / saddle.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 14, Differentiation and Gradients](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/14-differentiation-gradients/index.qmd)

### Hinge loss {#hinge-loss}

$\max(0, 1 - y \cdot \mathrm{score})$ — the loss minimised by soft-margin SVM. Zero past the margin; linearly penalising margin violations.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 31, SVM Primal](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/31-svm-primal/index.qmd)

### Hyperplane {#hyperplane}

A codimension-1 affine subspace; in $\mathbb{R}^n$, the solution set of a single linear equation $\boldsymbol{w}^\top \boldsymbol{x} = b$. The decision boundary of every linear classifier.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 06, Affine Spaces and Hyperplanes](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/06-affine-spaces/index.qmd)

### Inner product {#inner-product}

A symmetric, bilinear, positive-definite function $\langle \boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{v}\rangle$ on a vector space; induces a norm $\sqrt{\langle \boldsymbol{u}, \boldsymbol{u}\rangle}$ and an angle.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 07, Norms, Inner Products, Lengths, Angles](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/07-norms-inner-products/index.qmd)

### Jacobian {#jacobian}

For $\boldsymbol{g} : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}^m$, the matrix $\boldsymbol{J}_{ij} = \partial g_i / \partial x_j$ of partial derivatives. Generalises the derivative to vector-valued functions; multivariable chain rule = Jacobian product.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 14, Differentiation and Gradients](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/14-differentiation-gradients/index.qmd)

### KKT conditions {#kkt-conditions}

Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions: stationarity, primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness. Necessary at any smooth optimum of a constrained problem (under regularity); sufficient for convex problems.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 21, Constrained and Convex Optimisation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/21-constrained-convex-optimisation/index.qmd)

### Kernel (Mercer kernel) {#kernel-function}

A symmetric function $k(\boldsymbol{x}, \boldsymbol{x}')$ whose Gram matrix is positive semi-definite for every finite set of points; equivalent to an inner product in some feature space. The basis of the kernel trick.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 32, Dual SVM and Kernels](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/32-svm-dual-kernels/index.qmd)

### Kernel trick {#kernel-trick}

Replace inner products $\boldsymbol{x}^\top \boldsymbol{x}'$ in an algorithm with a kernel $k(\boldsymbol{x}, \boldsymbol{x}')$; the algorithm operates *as if* in a (possibly infinite-dimensional) feature space, without ever materialising features.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 32, Dual SVM and Kernels](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/32-svm-dual-kernels/index.qmd)

### Lagrange multiplier {#lagrange-multiplier}

The dual variable associated with a constraint in the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = f - \lambda h$; geometrically, the proportionality constant between $\nabla f$ and $\nabla h$ at a constrained extremum.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 21, Constrained and Convex Optimisation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/21-constrained-convex-optimisation/index.qmd)

### Latent variable {#latent-variable}

An unobserved random variable that, together with parameters, generates observed data. Examples: cluster assignment in a GMM, "true score" in factor analysis, $z$ in probabilistic PCA / VAE.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 28, PCA as a Latent-Variable Model](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/28-pca-latent-variable/index.qmd)

### Linear independence {#linear-independence}

Vectors $\boldsymbol{v}_1, \dots, \boldsymbol{v}_k$ are linearly independent if no non-trivial linear combination equals zero: $\sum \alpha_i \boldsymbol{v}_i = \boldsymbol{0}$ forces $\alpha_1 = \dots = \alpha_k = 0$.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 04, Linear Independence and Basis](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/04-linear-independence-basis/index.qmd)

### Linear mapping {#linear-mapping}

A function $\Phi : V \to W$ satisfying $\Phi(\alpha \boldsymbol{u} + \beta \boldsymbol{v}) = \alpha \Phi(\boldsymbol{u}) + \beta \Phi(\boldsymbol{v})$. Once a basis is chosen on each side, $\Phi$ is represented by a matrix.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 05, Rank and Linear Mappings](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/05-rank-and-linear-mappings/index.qmd)

### Mahalanobis distance {#mahalanobis-distance}

$\sqrt{(\boldsymbol{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1} (\boldsymbol{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})}$ — distance scaled by the inverse covariance; "natural" distance in Gaussian space.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 18, Summary Statistics and the Gaussian](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/18-gaussian-summary-stats/index.qmd)

### Marginal likelihood (evidence) {#marginal-likelihood}

$p(\mathcal{D} \mid M) = \int p(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta, M) p(\theta \mid M) d\theta$ — the probability of the data under model $M$ with parameters integrated out. Used for Bayesian model selection.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 23, Directed Graphical Models and Model Selection](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/23-graphical-models-model-selection/index.qmd)

### Maximum a Posteriori (MAP) {#map-estimator}

The mode of the posterior $p(\theta \mid D)$ — i.e. the most probable parameter given the data. Equivalent to regularised MLE with $-\log$ prior as the regulariser.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 25, Bayesian Linear Regression](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/25-bayesian-linear-regression/index.qmd)

### Multivariate Gaussian {#multivariate-gaussian}

$\mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{\mu}, \boldsymbol{\Sigma})$ in $\mathbb{R}^D$: density $\propto \exp(-\tfrac{1}{2}(\boldsymbol{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu})^\top \boldsymbol{\Sigma}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}))$. Closed under affine maps, marginalisation, and conditioning.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 18, Summary Statistics and the Gaussian](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/18-gaussian-summary-stats/index.qmd)

### Norm {#norm}

A length function $\lVert \cdot \rVert$ on a vector space, satisfying positive-definiteness, homogeneity, and triangle inequality. Common: L1, L2, L∞.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 07, Norms, Inner Products, Lengths, Angles](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/07-norms-inner-products/index.qmd)

### Normal equations {#normal-equations}

$\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} \boldsymbol{w} = \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$ — the linear system whose solution is the least-squares (MLE) regression weight.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 24, Linear Regression MLE](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/24-linear-regression-mle/index.qmd)

### Orthogonal complement {#orthogonal-complement}

For a subspace $U \subseteq V$, the set $U^\perp$ of vectors in $V$ orthogonal to every element of $U$. Always a subspace; $\dim U + \dim U^\perp = \dim V$.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 08, Orthonormal Basis and Orthogonal Complement](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/08-orthogonality/index.qmd)

### Orthogonal projection {#orthogonal-projection}

The unique map from $V$ to a subspace $U$ that returns the closest point in $U$ to the input; equivalent to "drop a perpendicular." Least-squares regression is orthogonal projection onto the column space.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 09, Orthogonal Projections and Rotations](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/09-projections-rotations/index.qmd)

### Orthonormal basis {#orthonormal-basis}

A basis of pairwise orthogonal unit vectors. Coordinates in such a basis are inner products with the basis vectors; sum of squared coordinates is squared norm (Parseval's identity).

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 08, Orthonormal Basis and Orthogonal Complement](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/08-orthogonality/index.qmd)

### Posterior {#posterior}

$p(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ — the conditional distribution of parameters given the data; obtained via Bayes' theorem from prior and likelihood.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 17, Probability Spaces and Bayes](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/17-probability-spaces-bayes/index.qmd)

### Principal Component Analysis (PCA) {#pca}

A dimensionality-reduction technique: find the directions (eigenvectors of the data covariance) that maximise variance — equivalently, minimise reconstruction error in projection.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 26, PCA Max-Variance and Projection-Error](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/26-pca-variance-projection/index.qmd)

### Probabilistic PCA {#probabilistic-pca}

Generative model with continuous latent $z \sim \mathcal{N}(\boldsymbol{0}, \boldsymbol{I})$ and observed $\boldsymbol{x} = \boldsymbol{W} z + \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$. MLE recovers classical PCA; gives posterior + sampling for free.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 28, PCA as a Latent-Variable Model](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/28-pca-latent-variable/index.qmd)

### Probability space {#probability-space}

A triple $(\Omega, \mathcal{F}, P)$: sample space, $\sigma$-algebra of events, probability measure. The formal setting of probability theory.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 17, Probability Spaces and Bayes](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/17-probability-spaces-bayes/index.qmd)

### Prior {#prior}

$p(\theta)$ — the distribution over parameters *before* observing the data. Updated via Bayes' theorem into a posterior.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 17, Probability Spaces and Bayes](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/17-probability-spaces-bayes/index.qmd)

### Pseudo-inverse {#pseudo-inverse}

$\boldsymbol{A}^+$ — for a matrix without a true inverse (non-square or singular), the minimum-norm least-squares solver. Computed from SVD: invert non-zero singular values, transpose.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 13, SVD and Low-Rank Approximation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/13-svd-and-low-rank/index.qmd)

### Rank {#rank}

The dimension of the column space (equivalently, the row space) of a matrix; the number of linearly independent columns. Invariant under change of basis.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 05, Rank and Linear Mappings](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/05-rank-and-linear-mappings/index.qmd)

### Regularisation {#regularisation}

A penalty $\lambda \Omega(\boldsymbol{w})$ added to the training loss to discourage extreme weight values; equivalent to a constraint on $\Omega(\boldsymbol{w})$. Ridge: $\Omega = \lVert \cdot \rVert_2^2$. Lasso: $\Omega = \lVert \cdot \rVert_1$.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 22, Data, Models, ERM](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/22-models-and-erm/index.qmd)

### Responsibility (GMM) {#responsibility}

$r_{nk} = p(z_n = k \mid \boldsymbol{x}_n)$ — the posterior probability that component $k$ generated point $\boldsymbol{x}_n$ in a Gaussian mixture model. Fractional / soft cluster assignment.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 29, Gaussian Mixture Models](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/29-gmm/index.qmd)

### Ridge regression {#ridge-regression}

Linear regression with $L_2$ penalty: $\min \lVert \boldsymbol{X}\boldsymbol{w} - \boldsymbol{y}\rVert^2 + \lambda \lVert \boldsymbol{w}\rVert^2$. Closed form $(\boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{X} + \lambda \boldsymbol{I})^{-1} \boldsymbol{X}^\top \boldsymbol{y}$ — MAP under a Gaussian prior on $\boldsymbol{w}$.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 25, Bayesian Linear Regression](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/25-bayesian-linear-regression/index.qmd)

### Rotation matrix {#rotation-matrix}

An orthogonal matrix with determinant $+1$; preserves lengths, angles, and orientation. In $\mathbb{R}^2$: $\begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix}$.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 09, Orthogonal Projections and Rotations](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/09-projections-rotations/index.qmd)

### Spectral theorem (MML view) {#spectral-theorem-mml}

Every real symmetric matrix has an orthonormal eigenbasis; equivalently, $\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\boldsymbol{Q}^\top$ with $\boldsymbol{Q}$ orthogonal and $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ diagonal real.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 12, Eigendecomposition and Diagonalisation](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/12-eigendecomposition/index.qmd)

### Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) {#sgd-mml}

Gradient descent where the gradient is estimated from a mini-batch rather than the whole dataset; unbiased, variance $\propto 1/B$. Cornerstone of large-scale ML training.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 20, Gradient Descent, SGD, Momentum](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/20-gradient-descent/index.qmd)

### Subspace {#subspace}

A non-empty subset of a vector space, closed under addition and scalar multiplication (equivalently: contains the origin and is closed under both operations).

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 03, Vector Spaces and Subspaces](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/03-vector-spaces/index.qmd)

### Sufficient statistic {#sufficient-statistic}

A function $\boldsymbol{T}(x)$ that summarises all information in the data relevant to inferring the parameter; in the exponential family, $\boldsymbol{T}$ appears in the density and the MLE depends on the data only through it.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 19, Conjugacy and the Exponential Family](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/19-conjugacy-change-of-variables/index.qmd)

### Support Vector Machine (SVM) {#svm}

Max-margin classifier: find the hyperplane separating two classes with the largest margin to the closest training points. Trained as a quadratic program; in the dual, depends on data only through inner products — enabling the kernel trick.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 31, SVM Primal](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/31-svm-primal/index.qmd)

### Support vector {#support-vector}

A training point exactly on the SVM margin (or inside it, for soft-margin); only support vectors contribute to the decision boundary.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 31, SVM Primal](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/31-svm-primal/index.qmd)

### Trace {#trace}

The sum of the diagonal entries of a square matrix; equal to the sum of its eigenvalues. Invariant under change of basis and under cyclic permutation of products.

**First named in:** [MML — Lesson 10, Determinant, Trace, Eigenvalues](lessons/tier-mml-ml-math/10-determinant-trace-eigen/index.qmd)

---

::: {.applied-callout}
**For authors.** Every term that a lesson formally introduces in its `## Name`
beat must also have an entry here. See the repo's authoring contract for the
exact rule and the entry template.
:::
